**Romania**[[a]](#cite_note-9) is a country in [Southeast](/wiki/Southeast_Europe "Southeast Europe") and [Central Europe](/wiki/Central_Europe "Central Europe"). It lies on the lower course of the [Danube](/wiki/Danube "Danube"), north of the [Balkan Peninsula](/wiki/Balkan_Peninsula "Balkan Peninsula"), and on the northwestern shore of the [Black Sea](/wiki/Black_Sea "Black Sea"). It borders [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine") to the north and east, [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary "Hungary") to the west, [Serbia](/wiki/Serbia "Serbia") to the southwest, [Bulgaria](/wiki/Bulgaria "Bulgaria") to the south, [Moldova](/wiki/Moldova "Moldova") to the east, and the [Black Sea](/wiki/Black_Sea "Black Sea") to the southeast. It is the [twelfth-largest country in Europe by area](/wiki/List_of_European_countries_by_area "List of European countries by area"), covering 238,397 km2 (92,046 mi2), and the sixth-most populous [member state of the European Union](/wiki/Member_state_of_the_European_Union "Member state of the European Union"), with nearly 20 million inhabitants. The capital, [largest city](/wiki/List_of_cities_and_towns_in_Romania "List of cities and towns in Romania") and [economic centre](/wiki/Economy_of_Romania "Economy of Romania") is [Bucharest](/wiki/Bucharest "Bucharest"). Other major cities include [Cluj-Napoca](/wiki/Cluj-Napoca "Cluj-Napoca"), [Iași](/wiki/Ia%C8%99i "Iași"), [Constanța](/wiki/Constan%C8%9Ba "Constanța"), [Timișoara](/wiki/Timi%C8%99oara "Timișoara"), [Brașov](/wiki/Bra%C8%99ov "Brașov"), [Oradea](/wiki/Oradea "Oradea") and [Sibiu](/wiki/Sibiu "Sibiu").

Romania was settled during the [Lower Paleolithic](/wiki/Lower_Paleolithic "Lower Paleolithic"), later becoming [Dacia](/wiki/Dacia "Dacia") before [Trajan's Dacian Wars](/wiki/Trajan%27s_Dacian_Wars "Trajan's Dacian Wars") and [Romanisation](/wiki/Romanization_(cultural) "Romanization (cultural)"). The modern Romanian state was formed in 1859 with [the unification](/wiki/Unification_of_Moldavia_and_Wallachia "Unification of Moldavia and Wallachia") of [Moldavia](/wiki/Moldavia "Moldavia") and [Wallachia](/wiki/Wallachia "Wallachia") under [Alexandru Ioan Cuza](/wiki/Alexandru_Ioan_Cuza "Alexandru Ioan Cuza"), becoming the [Kingdom of Romania](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Romania "Kingdom of Romania") in 1881 under [Carol I of Romania](/wiki/Carol_I_of_Romania "Carol I of Romania"). Romania [gained independence](/wiki/Romanian_War_of_Independence "Romanian War of Independence") from the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") in 1877, formalised by the [Treaty of Berlin](/wiki/Treaty_of_Berlin_(1878) "Treaty of Berlin (1878)"). After [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), [Transylvania](/wiki/Transylvania "Transylvania"), [Banat](/wiki/Banat "Banat"), [Bukovina](/wiki/Bukovina "Bukovina"), and [Bessarabia](/wiki/Bessarabia "Bessarabia") joined the [Old Kingdom](/wiki/Romanian_Old_Kingdom "Romanian Old Kingdom"), forming [Greater Romania](/wiki/Greater_Romania "Greater Romania"), which reached its largest territorial extent. In 1940, under [Axis](/wiki/Axis_powers "Axis powers") pressure, Romania lost territories to [Hungary](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hungary "Kingdom of Hungary"), [Bulgaria](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Bulgaria "Kingdom of Bulgaria"), and the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union"). Following the [Act of 23 August](/wiki/1944_Romanian_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "1944 Romanian coup d'état"), Romania switched sides to join [the Allies](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II"). After [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), it regained [Northern Transylvania](/wiki/Northern_Transylvania "Northern Transylvania") through the [Paris Peace Treaties](/wiki/Paris_Peace_Treaties "Paris Peace Treaties"). Under [Soviet occupation](/wiki/Soviet_occupation_of_Romania "Soviet occupation of Romania"), [King Michael I](/wiki/King_Michael_I "King Michael I") was [forced to abdicate](/wiki/Michael_I_of_Romania#Forced_abdication "Michael I of Romania"), and Romania became a [socialist republic](/wiki/Socialist_Republic_of_Romania "Socialist Republic of Romania") and [Warsaw Pact](/wiki/Warsaw_Pact "Warsaw Pact") member. After the [fall of the Iron Curtain](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Iron_Curtain "Fall of the Iron Curtain") and the [Romanian revolution](/wiki/Romanian_revolution "Romanian revolution") in 1989, Romania transitioned to a [liberal democracy](/wiki/Liberal_democracy "Liberal democracy").

Romania is a [developing country](/wiki/Developing_country "Developing country") with a [high-income economy](/wiki/High-income_economy "High-income economy") and is widely regarded as a [middle power](/wiki/Middle_power "Middle power") in [international relations](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_Romania "Foreign relations of Romania"). It is home to [11](/wiki/List_of_World_Heritage_Sites_in_Romania "List of World Heritage Sites in Romania") [UNESCO World Heritage Sites](/wiki/UNESCO_World_Heritage_Sites "UNESCO World Heritage Sites"). Romania is a [net exporter of automotive and vehicle parts](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_vehicle_exports "List of countries by vehicle exports") worldwide and has established a growing reputation as a [technology centre](/wiki/List_of_technology_centers "List of technology centers"), with some of the [fastest internet speeds globally](/wiki/List_of_sovereign_states_by_Internet_connection_speeds "List of sovereign states by Internet connection speeds"). Romania is a member of several [international organisations](/wiki/International_organisations "International organisations"), including the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"), [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO"), and the [BSEC](/wiki/BSEC "BSEC").

## Etymology

Main article: [Name of Romania](/wiki/Name_of_Romania "Name of Romania")

*Romania* derives from the local name for [Romanian](/wiki/Romanians "Romanians") ([Romanian](/wiki/Romanian_language "Romanian language"): *român*), which in turn derives from [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") [*romanus*](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/romanus "wikt:romanus"), meaning "[Roman](/wiki/Roman_people "Roman people")" or "of [Rome](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome")".[[9]](#cite_note-10) This ethnonym for Romanians is first attested in the sixteenth century by Italian humanists travelling in [Transylvania](/wiki/Transylvania "Transylvania"), [Moldavia](/wiki/Moldavia "Moldavia"), and [Wallachia](/wiki/Wallachia "Wallachia").[[10]](#cite_note-11)[[11]](#cite_note-12)[[12]](#cite_note-13) The oldest known surviving document written in [Romanian](/wiki/Romanian_language "Romanian language") that can be precisely dated, a 1521 letter known as the "[Letter of Neacșu from Câmpulung](/wiki/Neac%C8%99u%27s_letter "Neacșu's letter")",[[13]](#cite_note-14) is notable for including the first documented occurrence of *Romanian* in a country name: Wallachia is mentioned as *Țara Rumânească*.

## History

Main article: [History of Romania](/wiki/History_of_Romania "History of Romania")

Further information: [Timeline of Romanian history](/wiki/Timeline_of_Romanian_history "Timeline of Romanian history") and [Origin of the Romanians](/wiki/Origin_of_the_Romanians "Origin of the Romanians")

### Prehistory

Human remains found in [Peștera cu Oase](/wiki/Pe%C8%99tera_cu_Oase "Peștera cu Oase") ("Cave with Bones"), radiocarbon date from circa 40,000 years ago, and represent the oldest known *[Homo sapiens](/wiki/Homo_sapiens "Homo sapiens")* in Europe.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPrice201360–61-15) [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic") agriculture spread after the arrival of a mixed group of people from [Thessaly](/wiki/Thessaly "Thessaly") in the 6th millennium BC.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGeorgescu19911–2-16)[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPrice2013125–127-17) Excavations near a [salt spring](/wiki/Salt_spring "Salt spring") at [Lunca](/wiki/V%C3%A2n%C4%83tori-Neam%C8%9B "Vânători-Neamț") yielded the earliest evidence for salt exploitation in Europe; here salt production began between the 5th and 4th millennium BC.[[17]](#cite_note-18) The first permanent settlements developed into "proto-cities",[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPrice2013149-19) which were larger than 320 hectares (800 acres).[[19]](#cite_note-20)[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGeorgescu19912-21) The [Cucuteni–Trypillia culture](/wiki/Cucuteni%E2%80%93Trypillia_culture "Cucuteni–Trypillia culture")—the best known [archaeological culture](/wiki/Archaeological_culture "Archaeological culture") of [Old Europe](/wiki/Old_Europe_(archaeology) "Old Europe (archaeology)")—flourished in [Muntenia](/wiki/Muntenia "Muntenia"), southeastern Transylvania and northeastern Moldavia in the 3rd millennium BC.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGeorgescu19912-21)
The first fortified settlements appeared around 1800 BC, showing the militant character of [Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze_Age "Bronze Age") societies.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGeorgescu19912-21)

### Antiquity

Main article: [Romania in Antiquity](/wiki/Romania_in_Antiquity "Romania in Antiquity")

See also: [Legacy of the Roman Empire](/wiki/Legacy_of_the_Roman_Empire "Legacy of the Roman Empire")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Dacia_82_vChr.png/250px-Dacia_82_vChr.png)](/wiki/File:Dacia_82_vChr.png)

Maximum territorial extent of the [Kingdom of Dacia](/wiki/Dacia "Dacia") during [Burebista](/wiki/Burebista "Burebista")'s reign (early 40s BC)

Greek colonies established on the [Black Sea](/wiki/Black_Sea "Black Sea") coast in the 7th century BC became important centres of commerce with the local tribes.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGeorgescu19913-22)[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHitchins20143-23) Among the native peoples, [Herodotus](/wiki/Herodotus "Herodotus") listed the [Getae](/wiki/Getae "Getae") of the Lower Danube region, the [Agathyrsi](/wiki/Agathyrsi "Agathyrsi") of Transylvania and the [Syginnae](/wiki/Syginnae "Syginnae") of the plains along the river [Tisza](/wiki/Tisza "Tisza") at the beginning of the 5th century BC.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERustoiu200532,_35–36-24) Centuries later, [Strabo](/wiki/Strabo "Strabo") associated the Getae with the [Dacians](/wiki/Dacians "Dacians") who dominated the lands along the southern [Carpathian Mountains](/wiki/Carpathian_Mountains "Carpathian Mountains") in the 1st century BC.[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHitchins20147-25) [Burebista](/wiki/Burebista "Burebista") was the first Dacian ruler to unite the local tribes.[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHitchins20147-25)[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPop199914-26) He also conquered the Greek colonies in [Dobruja](/wiki/Dobruja "Dobruja") and the neighbouring peoples as far as the Middle Danube and the [Balkan Mountains](/wiki/Balkan_Mountains "Balkan Mountains") between around 55 and 44 BC.[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHitchins20147-25)[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPop199914–15-27) After Burebista was murdered in 44 BC, his kingdom collapsed.[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHitchins20147-25)[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGeorgescu19914-28)

The Romans reached [Dacia](/wiki/Dacia "Dacia") during Burebista's reign and conquered Dobruja in 46 AD.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGeorgescu19914-28) [Dacia](/wiki/Dacia "Dacia") was again united under [Decebalus](/wiki/Decebalus "Decebalus") around 85 AD.[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHitchins20147-25)[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPop199915-29) He resisted the Romans for decades, but the Roman army defeated his troops in 106 AD.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHitchins20149–10-30) Emperor [Trajan](/wiki/Trajan "Trajan") transformed [Banat](/wiki/Banat "Banat"), [Oltenia](/wiki/Oltenia "Oltenia") and the greater part of Transylvania into a new [province](/wiki/Roman_province "Roman province") called [Roman Dacia](/wiki/Roman_Dacia "Roman Dacia"), but Dacian, [Germanic](/wiki/Germanic_peoples "Germanic peoples") and [Sarmatian](/wiki/Sarmatian "Sarmatian") tribes continued to dominate the lands along the Roman frontiers.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGeorgescu19916-31)[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOpreanu200568–69,_97–98-32) The Romans pursued an organised colonisation policy, and the provincials enjoyed a long period of peace and prosperity in the 2nd century.[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPop199922–23-33)[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHitchins201413–14-34) Scholars accepting the Daco-Roman continuity theory—one of the main theories about the [origin of the Romanians](/wiki/Origin_of_the_Romanians "Origin of the Romanians")—say that the cohabitation of the native Dacians and the Roman colonists in Roman Dacia was the first phase of the Romanians' [ethnogenesis](/wiki/Ethnogenesis "Ethnogenesis").[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGeorgescu199110-35)[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPop199919–20-36) The [Carpians](/wiki/Carpians "Carpians"), [Goths](/wiki/Goths "Goths") and other neighbouring tribes made regular raids against Dacia from the 210s.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHitchins201414-37) The Romans could not resist, and Emperor [Aurelian](/wiki/Aurelian "Aurelian") ordered the evacuation of the province [Dacia Trajana](/wiki/Dacia_Trajana "Dacia Trajana") in the 270s.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOpreanu2005105–107-38) Scholars supporting the continuity theory are convinced that most Latin-speaking commoners stayed behind when the army and civil administration was withdrawn.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGeorgescu19918,_10-39) The Romans did not abandon their fortresses along the northern banks of the Lower Danube for decades, and Dobruja (known as [Scythia Minor](/wiki/Scythia_Minor "Scythia Minor")) remained an integral part of the Roman Empire until the early 7th century.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGeorgescu199110-35)[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOpreanu2005108,_110–111-40)

### Middle Ages

Main articles: [Romania in the Early Middle Ages](/wiki/Romania_in_the_Early_Middle_Ages "Romania in the Early Middle Ages"), [Romania in the Middle Ages](/wiki/Romania_in_the_Middle_Ages "Romania in the Middle Ages"), [Founding of Wallachia](/wiki/Founding_of_Wallachia "Founding of Wallachia"), and [Founding of Moldavia](/wiki/Founding_of_Moldavia "Founding of Moldavia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Gutthiuda.jpg/250px-Gutthiuda.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gutthiuda.jpg)

Gutthiuda, or the land of the [Gothic](/wiki/Gothic_language "Gothic language")-speaking [Thervingi](/wiki/Thervingi "Thervingi"), and the neighbouring tribes (370s AD)

The Goths were expanding towards the Lower Danube from the 230s, forcing the native peoples to flee to the Roman Empire or to accept their [suzerainty](/wiki/Suzerainty "Suzerainty").[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHitchins201416-41)[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeather2010116–117,_165-42)[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOpreanu2005117–118-43) The Goths' rule ended abruptly when the [Huns](/wiki/Huns "Huns") invaded their territory in 376, causing new waves of migrations.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHitchins201416-41)[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOpreanu2005117–118-43)[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeather2010151-44) The Huns forced the remnants of the local population into submission, but their empire collapsed in 454.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHitchins201416-41)[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeather2010151,_207–208-45) The [Gepids](/wiki/Gepids "Gepids") took possession of the former Dacia province.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBóna199466–67-46)[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECurta200654-47) Place names that are of [Slavic](/wiki/Slavic_languages "Slavic languages") origin abound in Romania, indicating that a significant Slavic-speaking population lived in the territory.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOpreanu2005131-48) The first [Slavic](/wiki/Early_Slavs "Early Slavs") groups settled in Moldavia and Wallachia in the 6th century,[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeather2010395–397-49) in Transylvania around 600.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBóna199497–99-50) The nomadic [Avars](/wiki/Pannonian_Avars "Pannonian Avars") defeated the Gepids and established a powerful empire around 570.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHitchins201416-41)[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECurta200662–63-51) The [Bulgars](/wiki/Bulgars "Bulgars"), who also came from the European [Pontic steppe](/wiki/Pontic_steppe "Pontic steppe"), occupied the Lower Danube region in 680.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHitchins201416-41)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Balkans850.png/250px-Balkans850.png)](/wiki/File:Balkans850.png)

[First Bulgarian Empire](/wiki/First_Bulgarian_Empire "First Bulgarian Empire") (681–1018) around 850

After the [Avar Khaganate](/wiki/Avar_Khaganate "Avar Khaganate") collapsed in the 790s, the [First Bulgarian Empire](/wiki/First_Bulgarian_Empire "First Bulgarian Empire") became the dominant power of the region, occupying lands as far as the river [Tisa](/wiki/Tisa "Tisa").[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHitchins201416-41) The [First Bulgarian Empire](/wiki/First_Bulgarian_Empire "First Bulgarian Empire") had a mixed population consisting of the Bulgar conquerors, [Slavs](/wiki/Slavs "Slavs") and [Vlachs](/wiki/Vlachs "Vlachs") (or Romanians) but the [Slavicisation](/wiki/Slavicisation "Slavicisation") of the Bulgar elite had already begun in the 9th century. Following the conquest of southern [Transylvania](/wiki/Transylvania "Transylvania") around 830, people from the Bulgar Empire mined salt at the local salt mines.[[51]](#cite_note-52) The [Council of Preslav](/wiki/Council_of_Preslav "Council of Preslav") declared [Old Church Slavonic](/wiki/Old_Church_Slavonic "Old Church Slavonic") the language of liturgy in the country in 893.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECurta2006168,_177-53) The Vlachs also adopted Old Church Slavonic as their liturgical language.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHitchins201437-54)

The [Magyars](/wiki/Magyars "Magyars") (or Hungarians) took control of the steppes north of the Lower Danube in the 830s, but the Bulgarians and the [Pechenegs](/wiki/Pechenegs "Pechenegs") jointly forced them to abandon this region for the [lowlands along the Middle Danube](/wiki/Great_Hungarian_Plain "Great Hungarian Plain") around 894.[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECurta2006123,_178-55) Centuries later, the *[Gesta Hungarorum](/wiki/Gesta_Hungarorum "Gesta Hungarorum")* wrote of the invading Magyars' wars against three dukes—[Glad](/wiki/Glad_(duke) "Glad (duke)"), [Menumorut](/wiki/Menumorut "Menumorut") and the Vlach [Gelou](/wiki/Gelou "Gelou")—for Banat, Crișana and Transylvania.[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHitchins201420-56)[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGeorgescu199114–15-57) The *Gesta* also listed many peoples—Slavs, Bulgarians, Vlachs, [Khazars](/wiki/Khazars "Khazars"), and [Székelys](/wiki/Sz%C3%A9kelys "Székelys")—inhabiting the same regions.[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESălăgean2005140-58)[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPop199936-59) The reliability of the *Gesta* is debated. Some scholars regard it as a basically accurate account, others describe it as a literary work filled with invented details.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECurta200615–16_(note_41)-60)[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESălăgean2005140–141-61)[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBóna1994111-62) The Pechenegs seized the lowlands abandoned by the Hungarians to the east of the Carpathians.[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECurta2006182–183-63)

[Byzantine](/wiki/Byzantine "Byzantine") missionaries proselytised in the lands east of the Tisa from the 940s[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECurta2006189–190-64) and Byzantine troops occupied Dobruja in the 970s.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESălăgean2005152-65) The first [king of Hungary](/wiki/King_of_Hungary "King of Hungary"), [Stephen I](/wiki/Stephen_I_of_Hungary "Stephen I of Hungary"), who supported Western European missionaries, defeated the local chieftains and established [Roman Catholic bishoprics](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_Romania "Catholic Church in Romania") (office of a bishop) in Transylvania and Banat in the early 11th century.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECurta2006248–250-66)[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPop199940–41-67) Significant Pecheneg groups fled to the Byzantine Empire in the 1040s; the [Oghuz Turks](/wiki/Oghuz_Turks "Oghuz Turks") followed them, and the nomadic [Cumans](/wiki/Cumans "Cumans") became the dominant power of the steppes in the 1060s.[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECurta2006304–305-68) Cooperation between the Cumans and the Vlachs against the Byzantine Empire is well documented from the end of the 11th century.[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESălăgean2005157-69) Scholars who reject the Daco-Roman continuity theory say that the first Vlach groups left their [Balkan](/wiki/Balkan "Balkan") homeland for the mountain pastures of the eastern and southern Carpathians in the 11th century, establishing the Romanians' presence in the lands to the north of the Lower Danube.[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBóna1994183-70)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Vlad_Tepes_002.jpg/250px-Vlad_Tepes_002.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vlad_Tepes_002.jpg)

[Vlad III of Wallachia](/wiki/Vlad_III_of_Wallachia "Vlad III of Wallachia") (also known as Vlad the Impaler), medieval ruler of Wallachia

Exposed to nomadic incursions, Transylvania developed into an important border province of the [Kingdom of Hungary](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hungary "Kingdom of Hungary").[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESălăgean2005158–159-71)[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBóna1994144–145-72) The Székelys—a community of free warriors—settled in central Transylvania around 1100 and moved to the easternmost regions around 1200.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPop199943-73) Colonists from the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire")—the [Transylvanian Saxons](/wiki/Transylvanian_Saxons "Transylvanian Saxons")' ancestors—came to the province in the 1150s.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPop199943-73)[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHitchins201421-74) A high-ranking royal official, styled [voivode](/wiki/Voivode_of_Transylvania "Voivode of Transylvania"), ruled the Transylvanian [counties](/wiki/Counties_of_Hungary_(before_1920) "Counties of Hungary (before 1920)") from the 1170s, but the Székely and Saxon [seats](/wiki/Seat_(territorial_administrative_unit) "Seat (territorial administrative unit)") (or districts) were not subject to the voivodes' authority.[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPop199941–43-75) Royal charters wrote of the "[Vlachs](/wiki/Vlachs "Vlachs")' land" in southern Transylvania in the early 13th century, indicating the existence of [autonomous Romanian communities](/wiki/Romanian_district "Romanian district").[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBóna1994189-76) Papal correspondence mentions the activities of Orthodox prelates among the Romanians in Muntenia in the 1230s.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECurta2006408-77) Also in the 13th century, the [Republic of Genoa](/wiki/Republic_of_Genoa "Republic of Genoa") started establishing [colonies](/wiki/Genoese_colonies "Genoese colonies") on the Black Sea, including [Calafat](/wiki/Calafat "Calafat"), and [Constanța](/wiki/Constan%C8%9Ba "Constanța").[[77]](#cite_note-78)[[78]](#cite_note-79)

The Mongols destroyed large territories during [their invasion of Eastern and Central Europe](/wiki/Mongol_invasion_of_Europe "Mongol invasion of Europe") in 1241 and 1242.[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPop199943–44-80) The Mongols' [Golden Horde](/wiki/Golden_Horde "Golden Horde") emerged as the dominant power of Eastern Europe, but [Béla IV](/wiki/B%C3%A9la_IV "Béla IV") of Hungary's land grant to the [Knights Hospitallers](/wiki/Knights_Hospitallers "Knights Hospitallers") in Oltenia and Muntenia shows that the local [Vlach](/wiki/Vlach "Vlach") rulers were subject to the king's authority in 1247.[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECurta2006407,_414-81)[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPop199944-82) [Basarab I of Wallachia](/wiki/Basarab_I_of_Wallachia "Basarab I of Wallachia") united the Romanian polities between the southern Carpathians and the Lower Danube in the 1310s.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPop199945-83) He defeated the Hungarian royal army in the [Battle of Posada](/wiki/Battle_of_Posada "Battle of Posada") and secured the independence of [Wallachia](/wiki/Wallachia "Wallachia") in 1330.[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPop199946-84)[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGeorgescu199117-85) The second Romanian principality, [Moldavia](/wiki/Moldavia "Moldavia"), achieved full autonomy during the reign of [Bogdan I](/wiki/Bogdan_I_of_Moldavia "Bogdan I of Moldavia") around 1360.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGeorgescu199117-85) A local dynasty ruled the [Despotate of Dobruja](/wiki/Despotate_of_Dobruja "Despotate of Dobruja") in the second half of the 14th century, but the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") took possession of the territory after 1388.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESălăgean2005202-86)

Princes [Mircea I](/wiki/Mircea_I_of_Wallachia "Mircea I of Wallachia") and [Vlad III of Wallachia](/wiki/Vlad_the_Impaler "Vlad the Impaler"), and [Stephen III of Moldavia](/wiki/Stephen_the_Great "Stephen the Great") defended their countries' independence against the Ottomans. Most Wallachian and Moldavian princes paid a regular tribute to the Ottoman sultans from 1417 and 1456, respectively.[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHitchins201426–29-87)[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPop199960–61,_63–66-88) [John Hunyadi](/wiki/John_Hunyadi "John Hunyadi"), organised the defence of the [Kingdom of Hungary](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hungary "Kingdom of Hungary") and [anti-Ottoman campaigns](/wiki/Hungarian%E2%80%93Ottoman_Wars "Hungarian–Ottoman Wars") from 1440 until his death in 1456.[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPop199961–62-89) Increasing taxes outraged the Transylvanian peasants, and [they rose up in an open rebellion](/wiki/Transylvanian_peasant_revolt "Transylvanian peasant revolt") in 1437, but the Hungarian nobles and the heads of the Saxon and Székely communities jointly suppressed their revolt.[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGeorgescu199130–31-90) The formal alliance of the Hungarian, Saxon, and Székely leaders, known as the [Union of the Three Nations](/wiki/Unio_Trium_Nationum "Unio Trium Nationum"), became an important element of the self-government of Transylvania.[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPop199952–53-91) The Orthodox Romanian *[knezes](/wiki/Knez_(Vlach_leader) "Knez (Vlach leader)")* ("chiefs") were excluded from the Union.[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPop199952–53-91)

### Early modern times and national awakening

Main articles: [Early Modern Romania](/wiki/Early_Modern_Romania "Early Modern Romania") and [Romanian War of Independence](/wiki/Romanian_War_of_Independence "Romanian War of Independence")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Moldavia%2C_Transylvania_and_Wallachia_under_Michael_the_Brave%27s_authority_%281600%29.png/250px-Moldavia%2C_Transylvania_and_Wallachia_under_Michael_the_Brave%27s_authority_%281600%29.png)](/wiki/File:Moldavia,_Transylvania_and_Wallachia_under_Michael_the_Brave%27s_authority_(1600).png)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/MihaiViteazul.jpg/120px-MihaiViteazul.jpg)](/wiki/File:MihaiViteazul.jpg)

During the [Long Turkish War](/wiki/Long_Turkish_War "Long Turkish War"), [Wallachian Prince](/wiki/List_of_rulers_of_Wallachia "List of rulers of Wallachia") [Michael the Brave](/wiki/Michael_the_Brave "Michael the Brave") (*portrayed*) briefly reigned over the three medieval principalities of [Wallachia](/wiki/Wallachia "Wallachia"), [Moldavia](/wiki/Moldavia "Moldavia"), and [Transylvania](/wiki/Transylvania "Transylvania"), covering most of the present-day territory of Romania

The Kingdom of Hungary collapsed, and the Ottomans occupied parts of [Banat](/wiki/Banat "Banat") and [Crișana](/wiki/Cri%C8%99ana "Crișana") in 1541.[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPop199952–53-91) Transylvania and [Maramureș](/wiki/Maramure%C8%99 "Maramureș"), along with the rest of Banat and Crișana, developed into a new state under Ottoman suzerainty, the [Principality of Transylvania](/wiki/Principality_of_Transylvania_(1570%E2%80%931711) "Principality of Transylvania (1570–1711)").[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGeorgescu199141-92) The [Reformation](/wiki/Reformation "Reformation"), initiated in Germany by [Martin Luther](/wiki/Martin_Luther "Martin Luther") in 1517, encouraged the spread of [Protestantism](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") across the region; by 1568, the [Edict of Torda](/wiki/Edict_of_Torda "Edict of Torda") formally granted local communities the right to choose their own preachers, sanctioning the existence of four "received" religions ([Catholicism](/wiki/Catholicism "Catholicism"), [Lutheranism](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism"), [Calvinism](/wiki/Calvinism "Calvinism"), and [Unitarianism](/wiki/Unitarian_Church_of_Transylvania "Unitarian Church of Transylvania")).[*[who?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*][[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPop199969-93) The Romanians' Orthodox faith remained only tolerated,[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPop199969-93) although they made up more than one-third of the population, according to 17th-century estimates.[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETrócsányiMiskolczy1994419-94)[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGeorgescu199171-95) In June 1609, [Gabriel Báthory](/wiki/Gabriel_B%C3%A1thory "Gabriel Báthory") freed the Romanian Orthodox clergy from both taxation and service demands.[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENagy1988290-96)

The princes of Transylvania, Wallachia, and Moldavia joined the [Holy League](/wiki/Holy_League_(1594) "Holy League (1594)") against the Ottoman Empire in 1594.[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGeorgescu199154-97) The Wallachian prince, [Michael the Brave](/wiki/Michael_the_Brave "Michael the Brave"), united the three principalities under his rule in May 1600.[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHitchins201435-98)[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGeorgescu199155–56-99) The neighbouring powers forced him to abdicate in September, but he became a symbol of the unification of the Romanian lands in the 19th century.[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHitchins201435-98) Although the rulers of the three principalities continued to pay tribute to the Ottomans, the most talented princes—[Gabriel Bethlen](/wiki/Gabriel_Bethlen "Gabriel Bethlen") of Transylvania, [Matei Basarab](/wiki/Matei_Basarab "Matei Basarab") of Wallachia, and [Vasile Lupu](/wiki/Vasile_Lupu "Vasile Lupu") of Moldavia—strengthened their autonomy.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPop199975–76-100)

The united armies of the [Holy League](/wiki/Holy_League_(1684) "Holy League (1684)") expelled the Ottoman troops from Central Europe between 1684 and 1699, and the Principality of Transylvania was integrated into the [Habsburg monarchy](/wiki/Habsburg_monarchy "Habsburg monarchy").[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPop199979-101) The Habsburgs supported the Catholic clergy and persuaded the Orthodox Romanian prelates to accept the [union with the Roman Catholic Church](/wiki/Church_union "Church union") in 1699.[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGeorgescu199142-102) In the [18th century](/wiki/18th_century "18th century"), [Moldavia](/wiki/Moldavia "Moldavia") and [Wallachia](/wiki/Wallachia "Wallachia") maintained their internal autonomy, but in [1711](/wiki/1711 "1711") and [1716](/wiki/1716 "1716"), respectively, the period of the [Phanariots](/wiki/Phanariots "Phanariots") began, with rulers appointed directly by the Porte from among the noble families of [Greek](/wiki/Greeks "Greeks") origin in [Constantinople](/wiki/Constantinople "Constantinople"). With the signing of the [Ausgleich](/wiki/Ausgleich "Ausgleich") in [1867](/wiki/1867 "1867"), [Transylvania](/wiki/Transylvania "Transylvania") quickly lost its remaining political autonomy, being politically and administratively incorporated into the [Kingdom of Hungary](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hungary "Kingdom of Hungary").[[102]](#cite_note-103) The Church Union strengthened the Romanian intellectuals' devotion to their Roman heritage.[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHitchins201460-104) The Orthodox Church was restored in Transylvania only after Orthodox monks stirred up revolts in 1744 and 1759.[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHitchins201461-105) The organisation of the [Transylvanian Military Frontier](/wiki/Transylvanian_Military_Frontier "Transylvanian Military Frontier") caused further disturbances, [especially among the Székelys in 1764](/wiki/Siculicidium "Siculicidium").[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETrócsányiMiskolczy1994432–434-106)

Princes [Dimitrie Cantemir](/wiki/Dimitrie_Cantemir "Dimitrie Cantemir") of Moldavia and [Constantin Brâncoveanu](/wiki/Constantin_Br%C3%A2ncoveanu "Constantin Brâncoveanu") of Wallachia concluded alliances with the Habsburg Monarchy and Russia against the Ottomans, but they were dethroned in 1711 and 1714, respectively.[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHitchins201444–45-107) The sultans lost confidence in the native princes and appointed Orthodox merchants from the [Phanar](/wiki/Phanar "Phanar") district of Istanbul to rule Moldavia and Wallachia.[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHitchins201455–56-108)[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGeorgescu199173–74-109) The [Phanariot](/wiki/Phanariot "Phanariot") princes pursued oppressive fiscal policies and dissolved the army.[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGeorgescu199174–75,_78-110) The neighbouring powers took advantage of the situation: the Habsburg Monarchy annexed the northwestern part of Moldavia, or [Bukovina](/wiki/Bukovina "Bukovina"), in 1775, and the [Russian Empire](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire") seized the eastern half of Moldavia, or [Bessarabia](/wiki/Bessarabia "Bessarabia"), in 1812.[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPop199992-111)[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGeorgescu199175–76-112)

A census revealed that the Romanians were more numerous than any other ethnic group in Transylvania in 1733, but legislation continued to use contemptuous adjectives (such as "tolerated" and "admitted") when referring to them.[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPop199987-113)[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETrócsányiMiskolczy1994427–428-114) The [Uniate bishop](/wiki/Romanian_Greek_Catholic_Church "Romanian Greek Catholic Church"), [Inocențiu Micu-Klein](/wiki/Inocen%C8%9Biu_Micu-Klein "Inocențiu Micu-Klein"), who demanded recognition of the Romanians as the fourth privileged nation, was forced into exile.[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGeorgescu199189–90-115)[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETrócsányiMiskolczy1994427–428-114) Uniate and Orthodox clerics and laymen jointly signed a [plea for the Transylvanian Romanians' emancipation](/wiki/Supplex_Libellus_Valachorum "Supplex Libellus Valachorum") in 1791, but the monarch and the local authorities refused to grant their requests.[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHitchins201473–74-116)[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPop199987-113)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/RomaniaBorderHistoryAnnimation_1859-2010.gif/250px-RomaniaBorderHistoryAnnimation_1859-2010.gif)](/wiki/File:RomaniaBorderHistoryAnnimation_1859-2010.gif)

Animated map depicting the territorial changes of Romania from [1859](/wiki/Unification_of_Moldavia_and_Wallachia "Unification of Moldavia and Wallachia") to [2010](/wiki/Maritime_Delimitation_in_the_Black_Sea_case "Maritime Delimitation in the Black Sea case")

The [Treaty of Küçük Kaynarca](/wiki/Treaty_of_K%C3%BC%C3%A7%C3%BCk_Kaynarca "Treaty of Küçük Kaynarca") authorised the Russian ambassador in Istanbul to defend the autonomy of Moldavia and Wallachia (known as the [Danubian Principalities](/wiki/Danubian_Principalities "Danubian Principalities")) in 1774.[[116]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHitchins201468-117) Taking advantage of the [Greek War of Independence](/wiki/Greek_War_of_Independence "Greek War of Independence"), a Wallachian lesser nobleman, Tudor Vladimirescu, stirred up a revolt against the Ottomans in January 1821, but he was murdered in June by Phanariot Greeks.[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGeorgescu1991103–104-118) After a [new Russo-Turkish War](/wiki/Russo-Turkish_War_(1828%E2%80%9329) "Russo-Turkish War (1828–29)"), the [Treaty of Adrianople](/wiki/Treaty_of_Adrianople_(1829) "Treaty of Adrianople (1829)") strengthened the autonomy of the Danubian Principalities in 1829, although it also acknowledged the sultan's right to confirm the election of the princes.[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHitchins201481-119)

[Mihail Kogălniceanu](/wiki/Mihail_Kog%C4%83lniceanu "Mihail Kogălniceanu"), [Nicolae Bălcescu](/wiki/Nicolae_B%C4%83lcescu "Nicolae Bălcescu") and other leaders of the [1848 revolutions in Moldavia](/wiki/Moldavian_Revolution_of_1848 "Moldavian Revolution of 1848") and [Wallachia](/wiki/Wallachian_Revolution_of_1848 "Wallachian Revolution of 1848") demanded the emancipation of the peasants and the union of the two principalities, but Russian and Ottoman troops crushed their revolt.[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPop199999-120)[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHitchins201496–97-121) The Wallachian revolutionists were the first to adopt the blue, yellow and red [tricolour](/wiki/Tricolour "Tricolour") as the [national flag](/wiki/Flag_of_Romania "Flag of Romania").[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPop1999100-122) In Transylvania, most Romanians supported the imperial government against the [Hungarian revolutionaries](/wiki/Hungarian_Revolution_of_1848 "Hungarian Revolution of 1848") after the Diet passed a law concerning the union of Transylvania and Hungary.[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPop1999100-122) Bishop [Andrei Șaguna](/wiki/Andrei_%C8%98aguna "Andrei Șaguna") proposed the unification of the Romanians of the Habsburg Monarchy in a separate duchy, but the central government refused to change the internal borders.[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHitchins201499-123)

### Unification and the Kingdom of Romania

Main articles: [Unification of Moldavia and Wallachia](/wiki/Unification_of_Moldavia_and_Wallachia "Unification of Moldavia and Wallachia"), [United Principalities of Moldavia and Wallachia](/wiki/United_Principalities_of_Moldavia_and_Wallachia "United Principalities of Moldavia and Wallachia"), and [Kingdom of Romania](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Romania "Kingdom of Romania")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/King_Carol_I_of_Romania_with_his_nephew_and_great_nephew.jpg/250px-King_Carol_I_of_Romania_with_his_nephew_and_great_nephew.jpg)](/wiki/File:King_Carol_I_of_Romania_with_his_nephew_and_great_nephew.jpg)

[King Carol I](/wiki/King_Carol_I "King Carol I") with his nephew [Ferdinand](/wiki/Ferdinand_I_of_Romania "Ferdinand I of Romania") and his son, [Carol II](/wiki/Carol_II "Carol II")

#### From the Little Union to the Great War

The modern Romanian state was created through the [unification of the principalities of Moldavia and Wallachia](/wiki/Unification_of_the_Romanian_Principalities "Unification of the Romanian Principalities"), accepted as a federative structure by the Great Powers following the [Paris Convention](/wiki/Paris_Convention "Paris Convention") of 1858, and later cemented by the simultaneous election as ruler of both states of the unionist [Alexandru Ioan Cuza](/wiki/Alexandru_Ioan_Cuza "Alexandru Ioan Cuza").[[123]](#cite_note-150_2012-07-06-124)[[124]](#cite_note-125)[[125]](#cite_note-126) After carrying out numerous reforms that laid the foundations for the modernisation of the state, he was forced in 1866 by a broad coalition of the political parties of the time, also known as the "[Monstrous coalition](/wiki/Monstrous_coalition "Monstrous coalition")", to abdicate and leave the country.[[126]](#cite_note-FC-127)

The union was at one time in peril, but the political leaders of the era succeeded in placing on the princely throne [Carol I of Romania](/wiki/Carol_I_of_Romania "Carol I of Romania"), who accepted the Constitution and took the oath on 10 May 1866. Eleven years later, on 10 May 1877, Romania proclaimed its independence—achieved on the battlefield—and in 1881, on the same day of the year, Carol was crowned as [King of Romania](/wiki/King_of_Romania "King of Romania"). In 1913, Romania entered the [Second Balkan War](/wiki/Second_Balkan_War "Second Balkan War") against [Bulgaria](/wiki/Bulgaria "Bulgaria"), at the end of which it obtained [Southern Dobruja](/wiki/Southern_Dobruja "Southern Dobruja").[[127]](#cite_note-128)[[128]](#cite_note-129)[[129]](#cite_note-130) In 1914, [King Carol I](/wiki/King_Carol_I "King Carol I") died, and his nephew, [Ferdinand I](/wiki/Ferdinand_I_of_Romania "Ferdinand I of Romania"), succeeded him on the throne.[[126]](#cite_note-FC-127)

#### World War I and the Great Union

Main article: [Romania in World War I](/wiki/Romania_in_World_War_I "Romania in World War I")

In 1916, Romania entered [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") on the side of the [Entente Powers](/wiki/Entente_Powers "Entente Powers").[[130]](#cite_note-131) Although the Romanian forces did not perform well militarily, by the end of the war the Austrian and Russian Empires had disintegrated; the National Assembly in [Transylvania](/wiki/Transylvania "Transylvania"), and the [Sfatul Țării](/wiki/Sfatul_%C8%9A%C4%83rii "Sfatul Țării") in [Bessarabia](/wiki/Bessarabia "Bessarabia") and [Bukovina](/wiki/Bukovina "Bukovina") proclaimed their union with Romania, and [King Ferdinand I](/wiki/Ferdinand_I_of_Romania "Ferdinand I of Romania") and [Queen Maria](/wiki/Queen_Maria_of_Romania "Queen Maria of Romania") were crowned sovereign of all Romanians in [Alba Iulia](/wiki/Alba_Iulia "Alba Iulia") on 15 October 1922.[[131]](#cite_note-132)

After [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), the union of [Bukovina](/wiki/Bukovina "Bukovina") with Romania was ratified in 1919 by the [Treaty of Saint Germain](/wiki/Treaty_of_Saint_Germain "Treaty of Saint Germain"). Most of the territories claimed by Romania from the [Kingdom of Hungary](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hungary "Kingdom of Hungary")—[Crișana](/wiki/Cri%C8%99ana "Crișana"), [Transylvania](/wiki/Transylvania "Transylvania") and parts of [Banat](/wiki/Banat "Banat") and [Maramureș](/wiki/Maramure%C8%99 "Maramureș")—were annexed to the [Kingdom of Romania](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Romania "Kingdom of Romania"). This act was ratified in 1920 by the [Treaty of Trianon](/wiki/Treaty_of_Trianon "Treaty of Trianon"), which defined the new border between Hungary and Romania.

#### Interwar period

After having left the country and renounced his claim to the throne in 1925, [Carol II](/wiki/Carol_II "Carol II") returned in 1930 and usurped his son's throne; influenced by his inner circle—referred to by historians as the "Royal Camarilla"—he gradually undermined the democratic system, and in 1938 he assumed dictatorial powers. Although he was pro-Western (especially Anglophile), Carol attempted to appease extreme centrifugal forces by appointing nationalist governments that adopted anti-Semitic measures, such as the [Goga cabinet](/wiki/Goga_cabinet "Goga cabinet") and the one led by the Orthodox Patriarch [Miron Cristea](/wiki/Miron_Cristea "Miron Cristea").

#### World War II

Main article: [Romania in World War II](/wiki/Romania_in_World_War_II "Romania in World War II")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/P%C3%A9rdidasTerritorialesRumanas1940-ro.svg/250px-P%C3%A9rdidasTerritorialesRumanas1940-ro.svg.png)](/wiki/File:P%C3%A9rdidasTerritorialesRumanas1940-ro.svg)

Romania's territorial losses in the summer of 1940. Of these territories, only [Northern Transylvania](/wiki/Northern_Transylvania "Northern Transylvania") was [regained](/wiki/Paris_Peace_Treaties,_1947 "Paris Peace Treaties, 1947") after the end of World War II

Following the [Molotov–Ribbentrop Pact](/wiki/Molotov%E2%80%93Ribbentrop_Pact "Molotov–Ribbentrop Pact") of 1939, in June 1940 Romania accepted the loss of [Bessarabia](/wiki/Bessarabia "Bessarabia"), [Northern Bukovina](/wiki/Northern_Bukovina "Northern Bukovina") and the [Hertsa](/wiki/Hertsa "Hertsa") region in favour of the [USSR](/wiki/USSR "USSR") (as stipulated in the Soviet ultimatum of 28 June 1940). Unaware of the details of the Soviet–German pact, Carol attempted to secure an alliance with [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany"), and appointed Ion Gigurtu as President of the Council of Ministers, who declared that he would pursue a Nazi pro-Axis (Berlin–Rome) policy that was anti-Semitic and fascist-totalitarian in nature.[[132]](#cite_note-133)[[133]](#cite_note-134)[[134]](#cite_note-135) Between 4 July and 4 September 1940, by accepting [Hitler's arbitration](/wiki/Second_Vienna_Award "Second Vienna Award") over Transylvania (after Gigurtu declared on radio that Romania must make territorial sacrifices to justify its Nazi orientation and full adherence to the Berlin–Rome Axis), Romania ceded [Northern Transylvania](/wiki/Northern_Transylvania "Northern Transylvania")—including the city of [Cluj](/wiki/Cluj "Cluj")—to Hungary.[[135]](#cite_note-136)[[136]](#cite_note-137)[[137]](#cite_note-138) The vast territories in Transylvania ceded by Ion Gigurtu to Hungary contained important natural resources, including gold mines.[[138]](#cite_note-139) Ion Gigurtu also initiated negotiations to cede 8,000 km2 of Southern Dobruja to Bulgaria,[[139]](#cite_note-140) these negotiations were interrupted by Antonescu's unconditional acceptance of the territorial cession.[[140]](#cite_note-romlib2018-07-04-141)

In response to the chaotic withdrawal from Bessarabia, the territorial cessions, public discontent, and protests from political leaders, King Carol II suspended the [1938 Constitution of Romania](/wiki/1938_Constitution_of_Romania "1938 Constitution of Romania") and appointed General [Ion Antonescu](/wiki/Ion_Antonescu "Ion Antonescu") as Prime Minister. This measure, supported by the [Iron Guard](/wiki/Iron_Guard "Iron Guard"), demanded that the king abdicate in favour of his son, [Mihai](/wiki/Mihai_I_of_Romania "Mihai I of Romania"). Subsequently, Antonescu assumed dictatorial powers and became President of the Council of Ministers, as the self-titled "Leader" of the state.[[141]](#cite_note-barba-142)[[142]](#cite_note-Encarta-143)

In 1941, as an ally of [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany"), Romania entered [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") by declaring war on the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union").[[141]](#cite_note-barba-142)[[142]](#cite_note-Encarta-143) A shift in fortunes only became discernible after the defeat at Stalingrad and the subsequent change of the USSR from a defensive to an offensive posture. On 23 August 1944, with the Soviet army having been present in northern Moldova since March, [King Mihai I](/wiki/Mihai_I_of_Romania "Mihai I of Romania") forcibly removed Marshal [Ion Antonescu](/wiki/Ion_Antonescu "Ion Antonescu") from power, as he refused to sign an armistice with the [Allies of World War II](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II").[[143]](#cite_note-Memoria-144) Following Antonescu's outright refusal, King Mihai I ordered the dismissal and arrest of the marshal, and Romania switched sides to join the Allies.[[143]](#cite_note-Memoria-144)

### Socialist Romania (1947–1989)

Main article: [Socialist Republic of Romania](/wiki/Socialist_Republic_of_Romania "Socialist Republic of Romania")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/Nicolae_Ceau%C8%99escu_%26_Kim_Il_Sung_%281978%29.jpg/250px-Nicolae_Ceau%C8%99escu_%26_Kim_Il_Sung_%281978%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nicolae_Ceau%C8%99escu_%26_Kim_Il_Sung_(1978).jpg)

[Nicolae Ceaușescu](/wiki/Nicolae_Ceau%C8%99escu "Nicolae Ceaușescu") with [Kim Il Sung](/wiki/Kim_Il_Sung "Kim Il Sung") of [North Korea](/wiki/North_Korea "North Korea") in 1978. In [reforming the state](/wiki/July_Theses "July Theses"), Ceaușescu sought to emulate *[Juche](/wiki/Juche "Juche")* and [Maoist](/wiki/Maoist "Maoist") ideas[[144]](#cite_note-Cioroianu_489-145)[[145]](#cite_note-Liiceanu_xviii-146)[[146]](#cite_note-Tismăneanu_241-147)

Less than three years after the [Soviet occupation of Romania](/wiki/Soviet_occupation_of_Romania "Soviet occupation of Romania"), in 1947, [King Michael I](/wiki/King_Michael_I "King Michael I") was forced to abdicate[[147]](#cite_note-148) and the [People's Republic of Romania](/wiki/People%27s_Republic_of_Romania "People's Republic of Romania")—a state of "popular democracy"—was proclaimed. The newly established communist regime, led by the [Romanian Workers' Party](/wiki/Romanian_Workers%27_Party "Romanian Workers' Party"), consolidated its power through a Stalinist-type policy aimed at suppressing any political opposition and transforming the economic and social structures of the old bourgeois regime.[[148]](#cite_note-CPADR-149)[[149]](#cite_note-150)

In the early 1960s, the Romanian government began asserting a certain degree of independence from the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") in its foreign policy,[[150]](#cite_note-ICIistoria-151) although it did not abandon its repressive policies (which it labeled "revolutionary conquests") in domestic affairs.[[148]](#cite_note-CPADR-149) In 1965, communist leader [Gheorghe Gheorghiu-Dej](/wiki/Gheorghe_Gheorghiu-Dej "Gheorghe Gheorghiu-Dej") died, ushering in a period of change in Romania.[[151]](#cite_note-HisBritan-152) After a brief power struggle, [Nicolae Ceaușescu](/wiki/Nicolae_Ceau%C8%99escu "Nicolae Ceaușescu") emerged as the head of the communist party,[[151]](#cite_note-HisBritan-152) becoming General Secretary of the [Romanian Communist Party](/wiki/Romanian_Communist_Party "Romanian Communist Party") in 1965, President of the [State Council](/wiki/State_Council_of_Romania "State Council of Romania") in 1967, and President of the [Socialist Republic of Romania](/wiki/Socialist_Republic_of_Romania "Socialist Republic of Romania") in 1974. Ceaușescu's rule from 1965 to 1989 grew increasingly authoritarian during the 1980s.[[150]](#cite_note-ICIistoria-151)

### Romania since 1989

Main article: [Romanian revolution](/wiki/Romanian_revolution "Romanian revolution")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Revolutia_Bucuresti_1989_000.JPG/250px-Revolutia_Bucuresti_1989_000.JPG)](/wiki/File:Revolutia_Bucuresti_1989_000.JPG)

Tanks and [Miliția](/wiki/Mili%C8%9Bia_(Romania) "Miliția (Romania)") on the [Magheru Boulevard](/wiki/Magheru_Boulevard "Magheru Boulevard") in [Bucharest](/wiki/Bucharest "Bucharest") during the revolution

In the context of the fall of communism throughout Eastern Europe during the [revolutions of 1989](/wiki/Revolutions_of_1989 "Revolutions of 1989"), a protest in support of [Reformed pastor](/wiki/Reformed_Church_in_Romania "Reformed Church in Romania") [László Tőkés](/wiki/L%C3%A1szl%C3%B3_T%C5%91k%C3%A9s "László Tőkés") that began in December 1989 in [Timișoara](/wiki/Timi%C8%99oara "Timișoara") quickly escalated into a national uprising against the communist regime, ultimately resulting in the [execution of Ceaușescu](/wiki/Trial_and_execution_of_Nicolae_and_Elena_Ceau%C8%99escu "Trial and execution of Nicolae and Elena Ceaușescu") and his wife [Elena](/wiki/Elena_Ceau%C8%99escu "Elena Ceaușescu") on 25 December 1989.[[152]](#cite_note-153)

An interim council composed of figures from civil society and former communist officials assumed control of the government, and [Ion Iliescu](/wiki/Ion_Iliescu "Ion Iliescu") became the provisional president of the country. The new government reversed many of the authoritarian communist policies[[153]](#cite_note-154)[[154]](#cite_note-155)[[155]](#cite_note-156) and dismissed several leaders of the former regime, although still influenced by members of the former regime (the basis for the [Golaniad](/wiki/Golaniad "Golaniad")*,* and [Mineriads](/wiki/Mineriads "Mineriads")).

In [May 1990](/wiki/1990_Romanian_general_election "1990 Romanian general election"), the first free elections in Romania since the [1937](/wiki/1937_Romanian_general_election "1937 Romanian general election") elections were held, with Iliescu of the [National Salvation Front](/wiki/National_Salvation_Front_(Romania) "National Salvation Front (Romania)") winning the presidency with 85% of the vote. In [1992](/wiki/1992_Romanian_general_election "1992 Romanian general election"), he was reelected in the first election after the adoption of [a permanent constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Romania "Constitution of Romania") via [a referendum](/wiki/1991_Romanian_constitutional_referendum "1991 Romanian constitutional referendum") held [the previous year](/wiki/1991_Romanian_constitutional_referendum "1991 Romanian constitutional referendum"). Illiescu lost the [1996 election](/wiki/1996_Romanian_general_election "1996 Romanian general election") to [Emil Constantinescu](/wiki/Emil_Constantinescu "Emil Constantinescu"), but returned to power in [2000](/wiki/2000_Romanian_general_election "2000 Romanian general election"). [Traian Băsescu](/wiki/Traian_B%C4%83sescu "Traian Băsescu") was elected president in [2004](/wiki/2004_Romanian_general_election "2004 Romanian general election") and [2009](/wiki/2009_Romanian_presidential_election "2009 Romanian presidential election"), serving until [2014](/wiki/2014_Romanian_presidential_election "2014 Romanian presidential election") at which point [Klaus Iohannis](/wiki/Klaus_Iohannis "Klaus Iohannis") succeeded him, being re-elected in [2019](/wiki/2019_Romanian_presidential_election "2019 Romanian presidential election") and serving until [2025](/wiki/2025_Romanian_presidential_election "2025 Romanian presidential election"). During these years several events occurred. In 2009, the country was bailed out by the [International Monetary Fund](/wiki/International_Monetary_Fund "International Monetary Fund") as result of the [Great Recession in Europe](/wiki/Great_Recession_in_Europe "Great Recession in Europe") following the [2008 financial crisis](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis").[[156]](#cite_note-igber-157)

The post-1989 period has been characterised by the privatisation and closure of several former industrial and economic enterprises from the communist period,[[157]](#cite_note-158) while [corruption](/wiki/Corruption_in_Romania "Corruption in Romania") has been a [major issue](/wiki/Corruption_in_Romania "Corruption in Romania") in contemporary politics.[[158]](#cite_note-159)

A [National Anticorruption Directorate](/wiki/National_Anticorruption_Directorate "National Anticorruption Directorate") was formed in the country in 2002.[[159]](#cite_note-160) During the 2000s, Romania had one of the highest economic growth rates in Europe and has been referred at times as "the Tiger of Eastern Europe".[[160]](#cite_note-autogenerated2-161) This has been accompanied by a significant improvement in living standards as the country successfully reduced domestic poverty and established a functional democratic state.[[161]](#cite_note-hdrstats.undp.org-162)[[162]](#cite_note-163) However, Romania's development suffered a major setback during the [late 2000s' recession](/wiki/Great_Recession "Great Recession") leading to a large gross domestic product contraction and a budget deficit in 2009.[[163]](#cite_note-wsj4dec09-164) This led to Romania borrowing from the International Monetary Fund.[[164]](#cite_note-165) Worsening economic conditions led to [unrest](/wiki/2012_Romanian_constitutional_crisis "2012 Romanian constitutional crisis") and triggered a political crisis in 2012.[[165]](#cite_note-166)

Since 2014, Romania launched an anti-corruption effort that led to the prosecution of medium- and high-level political, judicial and administrative offenses by the [National Anticorruption Directorate](/wiki/National_Anticorruption_Directorate "National Anticorruption Directorate").[[166]](#cite_note-:0-167) In 2015, massive [anti-corruption protests](/wiki/2015_Romanian_protests "2015 Romanian protests") which developed in the wake of the [Colectiv nightclub fire](/wiki/Colectiv_nightclub_fire "Colectiv nightclub fire") led to the resignation of prime minister [Victor Ponta](/wiki/Victor_Ponta "Victor Ponta").[[167]](#cite_note-168) During 2017–2019, in response to measures which were perceived to weaken the fight against corruption, some of the [biggest post-1989 protests](/wiki/2017%E2%80%932019_Romanian_protests "2017–2019 Romanian protests") took place in Romania, with over 500,000 people protesting nationwide.[[168]](#cite_note-169)[[169]](#cite_note-170)[[166]](#cite_note-:0-167)

The [2021 Romanian political crisis](/wiki/2021_Romanian_political_crisis "2021 Romanian political crisis") led to the ousting of [Florin Cîțu](/wiki/Florin_C%C3%AE%C8%9Bu "Florin Cîțu")'s [incumbent government](/wiki/C%C3%AE%C8%9Bu_Cabinet "Cîțu Cabinet"). The [Ciucă Cabinet](/wiki/Ciuc%C4%83_Cabinet "Ciucă Cabinet") then took power, with Romania since having experienced a shift towards [authoritarianism](/wiki/Authoritarianism "Authoritarianism") and [illiberalism](/wiki/Illiberalism "Illiberalism"),[[170]](#cite_note-171)[[171]](#cite_note-172)[[172]](#cite_note-173)[[173]](#cite_note-174) as well as an increased [corruption](/wiki/Corruption_in_Romania "Corruption in Romania"). Around this time, Romania was also hit by the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Romania "COVID-19 pandemic in Romania"). In the [2024 presidential election](/wiki/2024_Romanian_presidential_election "2024 Romanian presidential election"), Independent candidate [Călin Georgescu](/wiki/C%C4%83lin_Georgescu "Călin Georgescu") achieved a surprise win in the first round. However, the [Constitutional Court](/wiki/Constitutional_Court_of_Romania "Constitutional Court of Romania") annulled the election results, citing [Russian meddling](/wiki/Accusations_of_Russian_interference_in_the_2024_Romanian_presidential_election "Accusations of Russian interference in the 2024 Romanian presidential election"). The cancellation led to [far-right protests](/wiki/2024%E2%80%932025_Romanian_election_annulment_protests "2024–2025 Romanian election annulment protests"), [criticism](/wiki/2025_JD_Vance_speech_at_the_Munich_Security_Conference "2025 JD Vance speech at the Munich Security Conference") by the [Trump administration](/wiki/Second_presidency_of_Donald_Trump "Second presidency of Donald Trump"), and [Ilie Bolojan](/wiki/Ilie_Bolojan "Ilie Bolojan") becoming acting president in February 2025 as Iohannis resigned to political pressure.[[174]](#cite_note-Chao-Fong-175) In the subsequent [2025 Romanian presidential election](/wiki/2025_Romanian_presidential_election "2025 Romanian presidential election"), [Bucharest Mayor](/wiki/Mayor_of_Bucharest "Mayor of Bucharest") [Nicușor Dan](/wiki/Nicu%C8%99or_Dan "Nicușor Dan") was elected president.

## Geography

Main article: [Geography of Romania](/wiki/Geography_of_Romania "Geography of Romania")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/Romania_general_map-en.png/500px-Romania_general_map-en.png)](/wiki/File:Romania_general_map-en.png)

Topographic map of Romania

Romania is the largest country in [Southeastern Europe](/wiki/Southeastern_Europe "Southeastern Europe") and the [twelfth-largest](/wiki/List_of_European_countries_by_area "List of European countries by area") in Europe, having an area of 238,397 square kilometres (92,046 sq mi).[[175]](#cite_note-yearbook-176): 17 It lies between latitudes [43°](/wiki/43rd_parallel_north "43rd parallel north") and [49° N](/wiki/49%C2%B0_N "49° N") and longitudes [20°](/wiki/20th_meridian_east "20th meridian east") and [30° E](/wiki/30th_meridian_east "30th meridian east"). The terrain is distributed roughly equally between mountains, hills, and plains. The Carpathian Mountains dominate the centre of Romania, with [14 mountain ranges](/wiki/List_of_mountain_peaks_in_Romania "List of mountain peaks in Romania") reaching above 2,000 m or 6,600 ft—the highest is [Moldoveanu Peak](/wiki/Moldoveanu_Peak "Moldoveanu Peak") at 2,544 m or 8,346 ft.[[175]](#cite_note-yearbook-176): 11 They are surrounded by the [Moldavian](/wiki/Moldavian_Plateau "Moldavian Plateau") and [Transylvanian](/wiki/Transylvanian_Plateau "Transylvanian Plateau") plateaus, the [Pannonian Plain](/wiki/Pannonian_Plain "Pannonian Plain") and the [Wallachian](/wiki/Wallachian_Plain "Wallachian Plain") plains.

Romania is home to six terrestrial ecoregions: [Balkan mixed forests](/wiki/Balkan_mixed_forests "Balkan mixed forests"), [Central European mixed forests](/wiki/Central_European_mixed_forests "Central European mixed forests"), [East European forest steppe](/wiki/East_European_forest_steppe "East European forest steppe"), [Pannonian mixed forests](/wiki/Pannonian_mixed_forests "Pannonian mixed forests"), [Carpathian montane conifer forests](/wiki/Carpathian_montane_conifer_forests "Carpathian montane conifer forests"), and [Pontic steppe](/wiki/Pontic_steppe "Pontic steppe").[[176]](#cite_note-DinersteinOlson2017-177) Natural and semi-natural ecosystems cover about 47% of the country's land area.[[177]](#cite_note-biodiversity-178) There are almost 10,000 km2 (3,900 sq mi) (about 5% of the total area) of [protected areas in Romania](/wiki/Protected_areas_in_Romania "Protected areas in Romania") covering 13 [national parks](/wiki/National_park "National park") and three [biosphere](/wiki/Biosphere "Biosphere") reserves.[[178]](#cite_note-179) The [Danube](/wiki/Danube "Danube") river forms a large part of the border with [Serbia](/wiki/Serbia "Serbia") and [Bulgaria](/wiki/Bulgaria "Bulgaria"), and flows into the Black Sea, forming the Danube Delta, which is the second-largest and best-preserved delta in Europe, and a [biosphere reserve](/wiki/Biosphere_reserve "Biosphere reserve") and a biodiversity [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site").[[179]](#cite_note-180) At 5,800 km2 (2,200 sq mi),[[180]](#cite_note-181) the [Danube Delta](/wiki/Danube_Delta "Danube Delta") is the largest continuous marshland in Europe,[[181]](#cite_note-UNESCO-182) and supports 1,688 different plant species alone.[[182]](#cite_note-183)

Romania has one of the largest areas of undisturbed forest in Europe, covering almost 27% of its territory.[[183]](#cite_note-184) The country had a 2019 [Forest Landscape Integrity Index](/wiki/Forest_Landscape_Integrity_Index "Forest Landscape Integrity Index") mean score of 5.95/10, ranking it 90th globally out of 172 countries.[[184]](#cite_note-FLII-Supplementary-185) Some 3,700 [plant species](/wiki/Plant_species "Plant species") have been identified in the country, from which to date 23 have been declared [natural monuments](/wiki/Natural_monument "Natural monument"), 74 extinct, 39 endangered, 171 vulnerable, and 1,253 rare.[[185]](#cite_note-flora-186)

The [fauna of Romania](/wiki/Fauna_of_Romania "Fauna of Romania") consists of 33,792 species of animals, 33,085 [invertebrate](/wiki/Invertebrate "Invertebrate") and 707 [vertebrate](/wiki/Vertebrate "Vertebrate"),[[185]](#cite_note-flora-186) with almost 400 unique species of mammals, birds, reptiles, and amphibians,[[186]](#cite_note-187) including about 50% of Europe's (excluding Russia) [brown bears](/wiki/Brown_bear "Brown bear")[[187]](#cite_note-carnivoreconservation.org-188) and 20% of its [wolves](/wiki/Wolves "Wolves").[[188]](#cite_note-189)

### Climate

Main article: [Climate of Romania](/wiki/Climate_of_Romania "Climate of Romania")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/Romania_map_of_K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification.png/330px-Romania_map_of_K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification.png)](/wiki/File:Romania_map_of_K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification.png)

Romania map of Köppen climate classification, according with *Clima României* from the *Administrația Națională de Meteorologie*, Bucharest 2008

Owing to its distance from open sea and its position on the southeastern portion of the European continent, Romania has a climate that is [continental](/wiki/Continental_climate "Continental climate"), with four distinct seasons. The average annual temperature is 11 °C (52 °F) in the south and 8 °C (46 °F) in the north.[[189]](#cite_note-climate-190) In summer, average maximum temperatures in Bucharest rise to 28 °C (82 °F), and temperatures over 35 °C (95 °F) are fairly common in the lower-lying areas of the country.[[190]](#cite_note-clic.npolar.no-191) In winter, the average maximum temperature is below 2 °C (36 °F).[[190]](#cite_note-clic.npolar.no-191) Precipitation is average, with over 750 mm (30 in) per year only on the highest western mountains, while around Bucharest it drops to approximately 570 mm (22 in).[[175]](#cite_note-yearbook-176): 29
There are some regional differences: in western sections, such as Banat, the climate is milder and has some Mediterranean influences; the eastern part of the country has a more pronounced continental climate. In Dobruja, the Black Sea also exerts an influence over the region's climate.[[191]](#cite_note-192)

## Politics

Main article: [Politics of Romania](/wiki/Politics_of_Romania "Politics of Romania")

Romania is a [unitary](/wiki/Unitary_state "Unitary state") [semi-presidential](/wiki/Semi-presidential "Semi-presidential") [representative democratic](/wiki/Representative_democratic "Representative democratic") [republic](/wiki/Republic "Republic") with a structured system of governance and an active civil society. The [President](/wiki/President_of_Romania "President of Romania"), elected by popular vote, serves as the [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state"), representing the country in [international affairs](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_Romania "Foreign relations of Romania"), safeguarding [constitutional order](/wiki/Constitution_of_Romania "Constitution of Romania"), and acting as [supreme commander](/wiki/Commander-in-chief "Commander-in-chief") of the [Romanian Armed Forces](/wiki/Romanian_Armed_Forces "Romanian Armed Forces"). The [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Romania "Prime Minister of Romania"), appointed by the President and confirmed by the [Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Romania "Parliament of Romania"), acts as the [head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government"), responsible for overseeing the [executive branch](/wiki/Government_of_Romania "Government of Romania"), implementing [domestic and foreign policies](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_Romania "Foreign relations of Romania"), and managing [public administration](/wiki/Public_administration "Public administration"). [Legislative authority](/wiki/Legislative "Legislative") is vested in a [bicameral](/wiki/Bicameral "Bicameral") Parliament, consisting of the [Chamber of Deputies](/wiki/Chamber_of_Deputies_of_Romania "Chamber of Deputies of Romania") and the [Senate](/wiki/Senate_of_Romania "Senate of Romania"), whose members are elected through a [proportional representation](/wiki/Proportional_representation "Proportional representation") system. The [judiciary](/wiki/Judiciary_of_Romania "Judiciary of Romania") operates [independently](/wiki/Judicial_independence "Judicial independence"), with the [High Court of Cassation and Justice](/wiki/High_Court_of_Cassation_and_Justice "High Court of Cassation and Justice") as the highest court of appeal.

According to [International IDEA](/wiki/International_IDEA "International IDEA")’s Global State of Democracy (GSoD) Indices and Democracy Tracker, Romania performs in the mid- range on overall democratic measures, with particular strengths in inclusive suffrage and elected government.[[192]](#cite_note-193)[[193]](#cite_note-194)[[194]](#cite_note-195)

### Government

Main article: [Government of Romania](/wiki/Government_of_Romania "Government of Romania")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Nicu%C8%99or_Dan_-_Eastern_Flank_Summit_16_December_2025_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Nicu%C8%99or_Dan_-_Eastern_Flank_Summit_16_December_2025_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nicu%C8%99or_Dan_-_Eastern_Flank_Summit_16_December_2025_(cropped).jpg)

[Nicușor Dan](/wiki/Nicu%C8%99or_Dan "Nicușor Dan")  
[President](/wiki/President_of_Romania "President of Romania") since 2025

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/Ilie_Bolojan_-_6_March_2025_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Ilie_Bolojan_-_6_March_2025_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ilie_Bolojan_-_6_March_2025_(cropped).jpg)

[Ilie Bolojan](/wiki/Ilie_Bolojan "Ilie Bolojan")  
[Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Romania "Prime Minister of Romania") since 2025

Romania has a democratic, [multi-party system](/wiki/Multi-party_system "Multi-party system"), with [legislative power](/wiki/Legislative_power "Legislative power") vested in the government and the [two chambers](/wiki/Bicameralism "Bicameralism") of the [Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Romania "Parliament of Romania"), more specifically the [Chamber of Deputies](/wiki/Chamber_of_Deputies_(Romania) "Chamber of Deputies (Romania)") and the [Senate](/wiki/Senate_of_Romania "Senate of Romania"). The [judiciary](/wiki/Judiciary "Judiciary") is [independent](/wiki/Judicial_independence "Judicial independence") of the executive and the legislature. The latter is elected by popular vote for a maximum of two terms of five years and appoints the prime minister who in turn appoints the [Council of Ministers](/wiki/Romanian_Cabinet "Romanian Cabinet"). The legislative branch of the government, collectively known as the [Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Romania "Parliament of Romania") (residing at the [Palace of the Parliament](/wiki/Palace_of_the_Parliament "Palace of the Parliament")), consists of [two chambers](/wiki/Bicameralism "Bicameralism") ([Senate](/wiki/Senate_of_Romania "Senate of Romania") and [Chamber of Deputies](/wiki/Chamber_of_Deputies_(Romania) "Chamber of Deputies (Romania)")) whose members are elected every four years by [simple plurality](/wiki/Plurality_voting_system "Plurality voting system").[[195]](#cite_note-Europaworld-196)[[196]](#cite_note-197)

The justice system is independent of the other branches of government and is made up of a hierarchical system of courts with the [High Court of Cassation and Justice](/wiki/High_Court_of_Cassation_and_Justice "High Court of Cassation and Justice") being the supreme court of Romania.[[197]](#cite_note-198) There are also courts of appeal, county courts and local courts. The Romanian judicial system is strongly influenced by the [French model](/wiki/French_law "French law"), is based on [civil law](/wiki/Civil_law_(legal_system) "Civil law (legal system)") and is [inquisitorial](/wiki/Inquisitorial "Inquisitorial") in nature. The [Constitutional Court](/wiki/Curtea_Constitu%C8%9Bional%C4%83 "Curtea Constituțională") (*Curtea Constituțională*) is responsible for judging the compliance of laws and other state regulations with the constitution, which is the fundamental law of the country and can be amended only through a public referendum.[[195]](#cite_note-Europaworld-196)[[198]](#cite_note-199) Romania's 2007 entry into the EU has been a significant influence on its domestic policy, and including [judicial reforms](/wiki/Judicial_reform "Judicial reform"), increased judicial cooperation with other member states, and measures to combat corruption.[[199]](#cite_note-200) The [Economist Intelligence Unit](/wiki/Economist_Intelligence_Unit "Economist Intelligence Unit") rated Romania as a "[hybrid regime](/wiki/Hybrid_regime "Hybrid regime")" in 2024.[[200]](#cite_note-201)

### Foreign relations

Main article: [Foreign relations of Romania](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_Romania "Foreign relations of Romania")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Diplomatic_missions_of_Romania.PNG/330px-Diplomatic_missions_of_Romania.PNG)](/wiki/File:Diplomatic_missions_of_Romania.PNG)

[Diplomatic missions of Romania](/wiki/Diplomatic_missions_of_Romania "Diplomatic missions of Romania")

Countries that host a Romanian Embassy

Countries that do not host Romanian diplomatic missions

Romania

Since December 1989, Romania has pursued a policy of strengthening relations with the West in general, more specifically with the United States and the EU, albeit with limited [relations involving](/wiki/Romania%E2%80%93Russia_relations "Romania–Russia relations") the Russian Federation. It joined NATO on 29 March 2004, the EU on 1 January 2007, while it joined the International Monetary Fund and the World Bank in 1972, and is a founding member of the [World Trade Organization](/wiki/World_Trade_Organization "World Trade Organization").[[201]](#cite_note-202) Romania is recognised as a [middle power](/wiki/Middle_power "Middle power") for its military capabilities, as well as its active diplomatic engagement on the global stage.[[202]](#cite_note-HCSSABalancingAct-203)[[203]](#cite_note-StuckInMiddleGear-204)

Recent governments have stated that their goals include strengthening ties with and helping other countries (in particular [Moldova](/wiki/Moldova "Moldova"), [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine"), and [Georgia](/wiki/Georgia_(country) "Georgia (country)")) with better integration with the rest of the West.[[204]](#cite_note-mae-205) Romania has also made clear since the late 1990s that it supports NATO and EU membership for the democratic former Soviet republics in Eastern Europe and the [Caucasus](/wiki/Caucasus "Caucasus").

Romania applied to join to the [Schengen Area](/wiki/Schengen_Area "Schengen Area") in 2007, acquiring full membership in 2025 along with Bulgaria.[[205]](#cite_note-206)[[206]](#cite_note-207) In December 2005, President Traian Băsescu and [United States Secretary of State](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_State "United States Secretary of State") [Condoleezza Rice](/wiki/Condoleezza_Rice "Condoleezza Rice") signed an agreement that would allow a U.S. military presence at several Romanian facilities primarily in the eastern part of the country.[[207]](#cite_note-208) In 2009, US Secretary of State [Hillary Clinton](/wiki/Hillary_Clinton "Hillary Clinton") referred to Romania as "one of the most trustworthy and respectable" US allies".[[208]](#cite_note-209) However, by 2025, relations had worsen, with US vice president [JD Vance](/wiki/JD_Vance "JD Vance") in February [scolding](/wiki/2025_JD_Vance_speech_at_the_Munich_Security_Conference "2025 JD Vance speech at the Munich Security Conference") "[flimsy suspicions](/wiki/Accusations_of_Russian_interference_in_the_2024_Romanian_presidential_election "Accusations of Russian interference in the 2024 Romanian presidential election")" and "enormous pressure from its continental neighbours" for causing the annulment of the [2024 Romanian presidential](/wiki/2024_Romanian_presidential_election "2024 Romanian presidential election") election in which [Călin Georgescu](/wiki/C%C4%83lin_Georgescu "Călin Georgescu") won the first round.

[Relations with Moldova](/wiki/Romanian-Moldovan_relations "Romanian-Moldovan relations") are a special case given that the two countries share the same language and a [common history](/wiki/History_of_Moldavia "History of Moldavia").[[204]](#cite_note-mae-205) A [movement for unification of Moldova and Romania](/wiki/Movement_for_unification_of_Moldova_and_Romania "Movement for unification of Moldova and Romania") appeared in the early 1990s after both countries achieved emancipation from communist rule[[209]](#cite_note-cfis-210) but lost ground in the mid-1990s when a new Moldovan government pursued an agenda towards preserving a Moldovan republic independent of Romania.[[210]](#cite_note-Ihrig-211) After the [2009 protests in Moldova](/wiki/2009_Moldovan_protests "2009 Moldovan protests") and the subsequent removal of Communists from power, relations between the two countries have improved considerably.[[211]](#cite_note-212)

### Military

Main articles: [Romanian Armed Forces](/wiki/Romanian_Armed_Forces "Romanian Armed Forces") and [Military history of Romania](/wiki/Military_history_of_Romania "Military history of Romania")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Romanian_soldiers_at_the_Saber_Guardian_23_opening.jpg/250px-Romanian_soldiers_at_the_Saber_Guardian_23_opening.jpg)](/wiki/File:Romanian_soldiers_at_the_Saber_Guardian_23_opening.jpg)

Romanian soldiers at the Saber Guardian 23 exercise opening ceremony in [Smârdan, Galați](/wiki/Sm%C3%A2rdan,_Gala%C8%9Bi "Smârdan, Galați")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/TR-85M1_military_parade_2007.jpg/250px-TR-85M1_military_parade_2007.jpg)](/wiki/File:TR-85M1_military_parade_2007.jpg)

An TR-85 M1 tank at the 2007 Romanian military parade for national day, with the Arch of Triumph in the background

The Romanian Armed Forces consist of [land](/wiki/Romanian_Land_Forces "Romanian Land Forces"), [air](/wiki/Romanian_Air_Force "Romanian Air Force"), and [naval forces](/wiki/Romanian_Naval_Forces "Romanian Naval Forces") led by a [Commander-in-chief](/wiki/Chief_of_the_Romanian_General_Staff "Chief of the Romanian General Staff") under the supervision of the [Ministry of National Defence](/wiki/Ministry_of_National_Defence_(Romania) "Ministry of National Defence (Romania)"), and by the [president](/wiki/President_of_Romania "President of Romania") as the Supreme Commander during wartime. The Armed Forces consist of approximately 55,000 reservists and 71,500 active military personnel—35,800 for land, 10,700 for air, 6,600 for naval forces, and 16,500 in other fields.[[212]](#cite_note-military_balance_2022-213) Total defence spending in 2023 accounted for 2.44% of total national GDP, or approximately US$8.48 billion,[[213]](#cite_note-214) with a total of $9 billion intended to be spent until 2026 for modernisation and acquisition of new equipment.[[214]](#cite_note-215) Conscription stopped in 2007, when Romania switched to a volunteer army.

The Air Force operates [F-16AM/BM MLU](/wiki/General_Dynamics_F-16_Fighting_Falcon_variants#F-16AM/BM_Block_15_MLU "General Dynamics F-16 Fighting Falcon variants") fighters,[[215]](#cite_note-216) [C-27J Spartan](/wiki/C-27J_Spartan "C-27J Spartan") and [C-130 Hercules](/wiki/C-130_Hercules "C-130 Hercules") [transport aircraft](/wiki/Military_transport_aircraft "Military transport aircraft"), as well as [IAR 330](/wiki/IAR_330 "IAR 330") and [IAR 316](/wiki/IAR_316 "IAR 316") helicopters.[[216]](#cite_note-217) A [procurement programme](/wiki/Lockheed_Martin_F-35_Lightning_II_procurement#Romania "Lockheed Martin F-35 Lightning II procurement") for [F-35](/wiki/F-35 "F-35") [fifth-generation fighters](/wiki/Fifth-generation_fighter "Fifth-generation fighter") is also currently being carried out.[[217]](#cite_note-218) The Naval Forces operate three frigates, of which two are [Type 22 frigates](/wiki/Type_22_frigate "Type 22 frigate") acquired from the British [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy"),[[218]](#cite_note-awst_20061211-219) as well as four corvettes. The [River Flotilla](/wiki/Romanian_Danube_Flotilla "Romanian Danube Flotilla") operates [Mihail Kogălniceanu](/wiki/Mihail_Kog%C4%83lniceanu-class_river_monitor "Mihail Kogălniceanu-class river monitor") and [Smârdan-class river monitors](/wiki/Sm%C3%A2rdan-class_river_monitor "Smârdan-class river monitor").[[212]](#cite_note-military_balance_2022-213)

Romania contributed troops to the international coalition in [Afghanistan](/wiki/War_in_Afghanistan_(2001%E2%80%93present) "War in Afghanistan (2001–present)") beginning in 2002,[[219]](#cite_note-220) with a peak deployment of 1,600 troops in 2010 (which was the 4th largest contribution according to the US).[[220]](#cite_note-221)[[221]](#cite_note-222) Its combat mission in the country concluded in 2014.[[222]](#cite_note-223) Romanian troops participated in the [occupation of Iraq](/wiki/History_of_Iraq_(2003%E2%80%932011) "History of Iraq (2003–2011)"), reaching a peak of 730 soldiers before being slowly drawn down to 350 soldiers. Romania terminated its mission in Iraq and withdrew its last troops on 24 July 2009, among the last countries to do so.
