The frigate the *[Regele Ferdinand](/wiki/Regele_Ferdinand_frigate "Regele Ferdinand frigate")* participated in the [2011 military intervention in Libya](/wiki/2011_military_intervention_in_Libya "2011 military intervention in Libya").[[223]](#cite_note-224)

In December 2011, the Romanian Senate unanimously adopted the draft law ratifying the [Romania-United States agreement](/wiki/Romania%E2%80%93United_States_relations "Romania–United States relations") signed in September of the same year that would allow the establishment and operation of a US land-based [ballistic missile defence system](/wiki/Aegis_Ballistic_Missile_Defense_System "Aegis Ballistic Missile Defense System") in Romania as part of NATO's efforts to build a continental [missile shield](/wiki/NATO_missile_defence_system "NATO missile defence system").[[224]](#cite_note-225) The [Aegis Ashore](/wiki/Aegis_Ashore "Aegis Ashore") missile system based at [Deveslu](/wiki/Deveselu_Military_Base "Deveselu Military Base") became operational in 2016.[[225]](#cite_note-226)

In 2024, construction work started on expanding the Mihail Kogălniceanu Air Base ([RoAF 57th Air Base](/wiki/RoAF_57th_Air_Base "RoAF 57th Air Base")). The air base is set to become the largest NATO base in Europe after the implementation of a project spanning 20 years.[[226]](#cite_note-227)[[227]](#cite_note-228)

### Administrative divisions

Main article: [Administrative divisions of Romania](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_Romania "Administrative divisions of Romania")

Romania is divided into 41 [counties](/wiki/Counties_of_Romania "Counties of Romania") (*județe*) and the municipality of [Bucharest](/wiki/Bucharest "Bucharest"). Each county is administered by a county council, responsible for local affairs, as well as a [prefect](/wiki/Prefect_(Romania) "Prefect (Romania)") responsible for the administration of national affairs at the county level. The prefect is appointed by the central government but cannot be a member of any political party.[[228]](#cite_note-descopera-229) Each county is subdivided further into [cities](/wiki/Cities_of_Romania "Cities of Romania") and [communes](/wiki/Communes_of_Romania "Communes of Romania"), which have their own mayor and local council. There are a total of 320 cities and 2,861 communes in Romania.[[175]](#cite_note-yearbook-176): 17 A total of 103 of the larger cities have [municipality](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Romania "Municipalities of Romania") status, which gives them greater administrative power over local affairs. The municipality of Bucharest is a special case, as it enjoys a status on par to that of a county. It is further divided into six [sectors](/wiki/Sectors_of_Bucharest "Sectors of Bucharest")[[175]](#cite_note-yearbook-176): 6 and has a prefect, a general mayor (*primar general*), and a general city council.

The NUTS-3 ([Nomenclature of Territorial Units for Statistics](/wiki/Nomenclature_of_Territorial_Units_for_Statistics "Nomenclature of Territorial Units for Statistics")) level divisions of the EU reflect Romania's administrative-territorial structure and correspond to the 41 counties plus Bucharest.[[229]](#cite_note-nuts-230) The cities and communes correspond to the NUTS-5 level divisions, but there are no current NUTS-4 level divisions. The NUTS-1 (four [macroregions](/wiki/Macroregions_of_Romania "Macroregions of Romania")) and NUTS-2[[230]](#cite_note-LEGE_nr.151_din_15_iulie_1998-231) (eight [development regions](/wiki/Development_regions_of_Romania "Development regions of Romania")) divisions exist but have no administrative capacity and are used instead for coordinating regional development projects and statistical purposes.[[229]](#cite_note-nuts-230)

## Economy

Main article: [Economy of Romania](/wiki/Economy_of_Romania "Economy of Romania")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Bucharest_Business_District.jpg/250px-Bucharest_Business_District.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bucharest_Business_District.jpg)

[Floreasca](/wiki/Floreasca "Floreasca") business district, as seen from [Lake Herăstrău](/wiki/Lake_Her%C4%83str%C4%83u "Lake Herăstrău")

In 2024, Romania had a GDP (PPP) of around $894 billion and a [GDP per capita](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_per_capita_(PPP) "List of countries by GDP per capita (PPP)") ([PPP](/wiki/Purchasing_power_parity "Purchasing power parity")) of $47,203.[[6]](#cite_note-IMFWEO.RO-6) According to the World Bank, Romania is a [high-income economy](/wiki/High-income_economy "High-income economy").[[232]](#cite_note-WB_GROUP-233) According to [Eurostat](/wiki/Eurostat "Eurostat"), Romania's GDP per capita (PPS) was 77% of the EU average (100%) in 2022, an increase from 44% in 2007 (the year of Romania's accession to the EU), making Romania one of the fastest growing economies in the EU.[[233]](#cite_note-234)

The [Bucharest Stock Exchange](/wiki/Bucharest_Stock_Exchange "Bucharest Stock Exchange") (BVB) is the [stock exchange](/wiki/Stock_exchange "Stock exchange") of Romania, located in [Bucharest](/wiki/Bucharest "Bucharest"). In 2024, the BVB boasted a $74 billion [market capitalisation](/wiki/Market_capitalisation "Market capitalisation") and a [trading volume](/wiki/Trading_volume "Trading volume") of $7.2 billion.[[234]](#cite_note-General_statistics-235) As of 2024, 86 companies were listed on the exchange.[[234]](#cite_note-General_statistics-235) In September 2020, [FTSE Russell](/wiki/FTSE_Russell "FTSE Russell") upgraded the BVB from a [Frontier market](/wiki/Frontier_market "Frontier market") to a [Secondary Emerging Market](/wiki/Emerging_market "Emerging market").[[235]](#cite_note-236)

After 1989 the country experienced a decade of economic instability and decline, led in part by an obsolete industrial base and a lack of structural reform. From 2000 onwards, however, the Romanian economy was transformed into one of relative [macroeconomic](/wiki/Macroeconomic "Macroeconomic") stability, characterised by high growth, low unemployment and declining inflation. In 2006, according to the [Romanian Statistics Office](/wiki/National_Institute_of_Statistics_(Romania) "National Institute of Statistics (Romania)"), GDP growth in real terms was recorded at 7.7%, one of the highest rates in Europe.[[236]](#cite_note-237) However, the [Great Recession](/wiki/Great_Recession "Great Recession") forced the government to borrow externally, including an [IMF](/wiki/IMF "IMF") €20 billion bailout programme.[[237]](#cite_note-238) According to [The World Bank](/wiki/The_World_Bank "The World Bank"), GDP per capita in purchasing power parity grew from $13,703 in 2007 to $47,903 in 2023.[[238]](#cite_note-239)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Cladire_Bursa_de_Valori_Bucuresti.jpg/250px-Cladire_Bursa_de_Valori_Bucuresti.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cladire_Bursa_de_Valori_Bucuresti.jpg)

The [Bucharest Stock Exchange](/wiki/Bucharest_Stock_Exchange "Bucharest Stock Exchange") tower, a key institution in Romania's [financial sector](/wiki/Financial_sector "Financial sector")

Romania's main exports are [vehicles](/wiki/Vehicles "Vehicles"), [software](/wiki/Software "Software"), [clothing](/wiki/Clothing "Clothing") and [textiles](/wiki/Textiles "Textiles"), [industrial machinery](/wiki/Machine_industry "Machine industry"), [electrical](/wiki/Electrical_device "Electrical device") and [electronic equipment](/wiki/Electronic_equipment "Electronic equipment"), [metallurgic products](/wiki/Metallurgy "Metallurgy"), [raw materials](/wiki/Raw_materials "Raw materials"), [military equipment](/wiki/Military_equipment "Military equipment"), [pharmaceuticals](/wiki/Pharmaceuticals "Pharmaceuticals"), [fine chemicals](/wiki/Fine_chemicals "Fine chemicals"), and [agricultural products](/wiki/Agriculture "Agriculture") (fruits, vegetables, and flowers). Trade is mostly centred on the member states of the EU, with Germany, Italy and France being the country's single largest trading partners.

In 2005, the government replaced Romania's [progressive tax](/wiki/Progressive_tax "Progressive tax") system with a [flat tax](/wiki/Flat_tax "Flat tax") of 16% for both personal income and corporate profit, among the lowest rates in the EU.[[239]](#cite_note-240) The economy is based predominantly on services, which account for 56.2% of the country's total GDP as of 2017, with industry and agriculture accounting for 30% and 4.4% respectively.[[240]](#cite_note-241)
Approximately 25.8% of the Romanian workforce is employed in agriculture, one of the highest rates in Europe.[[241]](#cite_note-242)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/Dacia_Logan_2023_Front_2.jpg/250px-Dacia_Logan_2023_Front_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dacia_Logan_2023_Front_2.jpg)

Romania is home to automobile company [Dacia](/wiki/Automobile_Dacia "Automobile Dacia")

Romania has attracted increasing amounts of foreign investment following the end of communism, with the stock of [foreign direct investment](/wiki/Foreign_direct_investment "Foreign direct investment") (FDI) in Romania rising to €83.8 billion in June 2019.[[242]](#cite_note-business-review.eu-243) Romania's FDI outward stock (an external or foreign business either investing in or purchasing the stock of a local economy) amounted to $745 million in December 2018, the lowest value among the 28 EU member states.[[242]](#cite_note-business-review.eu-243)

Since 1867 the official currency has been the [Romanian *leu*](/wiki/Romanian_leu "Romanian leu") ("lion"), which "was [redenominated](/wiki/Redenomination "Redenomination"), with the exchange rate set at 1 new leu = 10,000 old lei" in 2005 according to the [National Bank of Romania](/wiki/National_Bank_of_Romania "National Bank of Romania").[[243]](#cite_note-244) As it joined the EU in 2007, Romania plans to adopt the [euro](/wiki/Euro "Euro") in 2029.[[244]](#cite_note-245)

### Infrastructure

Main articles: [Transport in Romania](/wiki/Transport_in_Romania "Transport in Romania") and [Energy in Romania](/wiki/Energy_in_Romania "Energy in Romania")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Romania-drumuri.svg/250px-Romania-drumuri.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Romania-drumuri.svg)

Romania's [road network](/wiki/Roads_in_Romania "Roads in Romania")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Graph_Romania_electricity_supply_mix_2015.svg/250px-Graph_Romania_electricity_supply_mix_2015.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Graph_Romania_electricity_supply_mix_2015.svg)

Graph depicting Romania's electricity supply mix as of 2015

According to the Romania's National Institute of Statistics (INS), Romania's total road network was estimated in 2015 at 86,080 kilometres (53,488 mi).[[245]](#cite_note-INSSE-246) The World Bank estimates the railway network at 22,298 kilometres (13,855 mi) of track, the fourth-largest railway network in Europe.[[246]](#cite_note-Railway-247) Romania's [rail transport](/wiki/C%C4%83ile_Ferate_Rom%C3%A2ne "Căile Ferate Române") experienced a dramatic decline after 1989 and was estimated at 99 million passenger journeys in 2004, but has experienced a recent (2013) revival due to infrastructure improvements and partial privatisation of lines,[[195]](#cite_note-Europaworld-196) accounting for 45% of all passenger and freight movements in the country.[[195]](#cite_note-Europaworld-196) [Bucharest Metro](/wiki/Bucharest_Metro "Bucharest Metro"), the only [underground](/wiki/Rapid_transit "Rapid transit") railway system, was opened in 1979 and measures 80.01 km (49.72 mi) with an average ridership in 2021 of 720,000 passengers during the workweek in the country.[[247]](#cite_note-248) There are [sixteen international commercial airports](/wiki/List_of_airports_in_Romania "List of airports in Romania") in service today. Over 12.8 million passengers flew through Bucharest's [Henri Coandă International Airport](/wiki/Henri_Coand%C4%83_International_Airport "Henri Coandă International Airport") in 2017.[[248]](#cite_note-249)

Romania is a net exporter of electrical energy and is 52nd worldwide in terms of consumption of electric energy.[[249]](#cite_note-250) Around a third of the produced energy comes from renewable sources, mostly as hydroelectric power.[[250]](#cite_note-251) It has one of the largest refining capacities in Eastern Europe, even though oil and natural gas production has been decreasing for more than a decade.[[251]](#cite_note-252) With one of the largest reserves of [crude oil](/wiki/Crude_oil "Crude oil") and [shale gas](/wiki/Shale_gas "Shale gas") in Europe[[252]](#cite_note-253) it is among the most energy-independent countries in the EU,[[253]](#cite_note-254) and is looking to expand its nuclear power plant at [Cernavodă](/wiki/Cernavod%C4%83_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Cernavodă Nuclear Power Plant") further.[[254]](#cite_note-255)

There were almost 18.3 million connections to the Internet in June 2014.[[255]](#cite_note-256) According to [Bloomberg](/wiki/Bloomberg_L.P. "Bloomberg L.P."), in 2013 Romania ranked fifth in the world, and according to *[The Independent](/wiki/The_Independent "The Independent")*, it ranks number one in Europe at Internet speeds,[[256]](#cite_note-257)[[257]](#cite_note-258) with [Timișoara](/wiki/Timi%C8%99oara "Timișoara") ranked among the highest in the world.[[258]](#cite_note-Akamai-259)

### Tourism

Main articles: [Tourism in Romania](/wiki/Tourism_in_Romania "Tourism in Romania") and [List of World Heritage Sites in Romania](/wiki/List_of_World_Heritage_Sites_in_Romania "List of World Heritage Sites in Romania")

See also: [Seven Natural Wonders of Romania](/wiki/Seven_Natural_Wonders_of_Romania "Seven Natural Wonders of Romania") and [Seven Wonders of Romania](/wiki/Seven_Wonders_of_Romania "Seven Wonders of Romania")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/Manastirea_putna1.jpg/250px-Manastirea_putna1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Manastirea_putna1.jpg)

[Putna Monastery](/wiki/Putna_Monastery "Putna Monastery") in [Bukovina](/wiki/Bukovina "Bukovina"), one of the [medieval churches of Moldavia](/wiki/Churches_of_Moldavia "Churches of Moldavia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Pelicani_din_Delta_Dunarii.PNG/250px-Pelicani_din_Delta_Dunarii.PNG)](/wiki/File:Pelicani_din_Delta_Dunarii.PNG)

The [Danube Delta](/wiki/Danube_Delta "Danube Delta") with its wildlife

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Castelul_Bran2.jpg/250px-Castelul_Bran2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Castelul_Bran2.jpg)

[Bran Castle](/wiki/Bran_Castle "Bran Castle")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/Black_Sea_beach_in_Mamaia_%286117712232%29.jpg/250px-Black_Sea_beach_in_Mamaia_%286117712232%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Black_Sea_beach_in_Mamaia_(6117712232).jpg)

[Mamaia Black Sea resort](/wiki/Romanian_Black_Sea_resorts "Romanian Black Sea resorts")

Tourism is a significant contributor to the Romanian economy, generating around 5% of GDP.[[259]](#cite_note-260) The number of tourists has been rising steadily, reaching 9.33 million foreign tourists in 2016, according to the Worldbank.[[260]](#cite_note-business_review_website-261) Tourism in Romania attracted €400 million in investments in 2005.[[261]](#cite_note-262) More than 60% of the foreign visitors in 2007 were from other EU countries.[[262]](#cite_note-turism-263) The popular summer attractions of [Mamaia](/wiki/Mamaia "Mamaia") and other [Black Sea Resorts](/wiki/Romanian_Black_Sea_resorts "Romanian Black Sea resorts") attracted 1.3 million tourists in 2009.[[263]](#cite_note-litoral2010-08-21-264)[[264]](#cite_note-265)

Most popular skiing resorts are along the [Valea Prahovei](/wiki/Valea_Prahovei "Valea Prahovei") and in [Poiana Brașov](/wiki/Poiana_Bra%C8%99ov "Poiana Brașov"). [Castles, fortifications, or strongholds](/wiki/List_of_castles_in_Romania "List of castles in Romania") as well as preserved medieval Transylvanian cities or towns such as [Cluj-Napoca](/wiki/Cluj-Napoca "Cluj-Napoca"), [Sibiu](/wiki/Sibiu "Sibiu"), [Brașov](/wiki/Bra%C8%99ov "Brașov"), [Alba Iulia](/wiki/Alba_Iulia "Alba Iulia"), [Baia Mare](/wiki/Baia_Mare "Baia Mare"), [Bistrița](/wiki/Bistri%C8%9Ba "Bistrița"), [Mediaș](/wiki/Media%C8%99 "Mediaș"), [Cisnădie](/wiki/Cisn%C4%83die "Cisnădie"), [Sebeș](/wiki/Sebe%C8%99 "Sebeș"), or [Sighișoara](/wiki/Sighi%C8%99oara "Sighișoara") also attract a large number of tourists. [Bran Castle](/wiki/Bran_Castle "Bran Castle"), near Brașov, is one of the most famous attractions in Romania, drawing hundreds of thousands of tourists every year as it is often (falsely) advertised as being [Dracula](/wiki/Dracula_in_popular_culture "Dracula in popular culture")'s Castle.[[265]](#cite_note-266) Other attractions include the Danube Delta or the [Sculptural Ensemble of Constantin Brâncuși at Târgu Jiu](/wiki/Sculptural_Ensemble_of_Constantin_Br%C3%A2ncu%C8%99i_at_T%C3%A2rgu_Jiu "Sculptural Ensemble of Constantin Brâncuși at Târgu Jiu").[[266]](#cite_note-267)[[267]](#cite_note-268)

[Rural tourism](/wiki/Rural_tourism "Rural tourism"), focusing on getting visitors acquainted with local [folklore](/wiki/Folklore "Folklore") and [customs](/wiki/Tradition "Tradition"), has become an important alternative,[[268]](#cite_note-269) and is targeted to promote such sites as [Bran](/wiki/Bran,_Bra%C8%99ov "Bran, Brașov") and its Dracula's Castle, the [painted churches of northern Moldavia](/wiki/Painted_churches_of_northern_Moldavia "Painted churches of northern Moldavia"), and the [wooden churches of Maramureș](/wiki/Wooden_churches_of_Maramure%C8%99 "Wooden churches of Maramureș"), or the [villages with fortified churches in Transylvania](/wiki/Villages_with_fortified_churches_in_Transylvania "Villages with fortified churches in Transylvania").[[269]](#cite_note-270) The [Via Transilvanica](/wiki/Via_Transilvanica "Via Transilvanica") long-distance [hiking](/wiki/Hiking "Hiking") and [cycling](/wiki/Cycling "Cycling") [trail](/wiki/Trail "Trail"), which crosses 10 counties in the [Transylvania](/wiki/Transylvania "Transylvania"), [Banat](/wiki/Banat "Banat") and [Bukovina](/wiki/Bukovina "Bukovina") regions of the country further promotes rural [slow tourism](/wiki/Slow_tourism "Slow tourism").[[270]](#cite_note-271)

In 2014, Romania had 32,500 companies active in the hotel and restaurant industry, with a total turnover of €2.6 billion.[[271]](#cite_note-272) More than 1.9 million foreign tourists visited Romania in 2014, 12% more than in 2013.[[272]](#cite_note-romania-insider.com-273) According to the country's National Statistics Institute, some 77% came from Europe (particularly from Germany, Italy, and France), 12% from Asia, and less than 7% from North America.[[272]](#cite_note-romania-insider.com-273)

### Science and technology

Main articles: [Science and technology in Romania](/wiki/Science_and_technology_in_Romania "Science and technology in Romania") and [List of Romanian inventors and discoverers](/wiki/List_of_Romanian_inventors_and_discoverers "List of Romanian inventors and discoverers")

Historically, Romanian researchers and inventors have made notable contributions to several fields. In the history of flight, [Traian Vuia](/wiki/Traian_Vuia "Traian Vuia") built the first [aeroplane](/wiki/Aeroplane "Aeroplane") to take off under its own power[[273]](#cite_note-RomAcademyLib-274) and [Aurel Vlaicu](/wiki/Aurel_Vlaicu "Aurel Vlaicu") built and flew some of the earliest successful aircraft,[[274]](#cite_note-275) while [Henri Coandă](/wiki/Henri_Coand%C4%83 "Henri Coandă") discovered the [Coandă effect](/wiki/Coand%C4%83_effect "Coandă effect") of fluidics.[[275]](#cite_note-276) [Victor Babeș](/wiki/Victor_Babe%C8%99 "Victor Babeș") discovered more than 50 types of bacteria;[[276]](#cite_note-277) biologist [Nicolae Paulescu](/wiki/Nicolae_Paulescu "Nicolae Paulescu") developed an extract of the pancreas and showed that it lowers blood sugar in diabetic dogs, thus being significant in the history of insulin;[[277]](#cite_note-278) while [Emil Palade](/wiki/Emil_Palade "Emil Palade") received the Nobel Prize for his contributions to [cell biology](/wiki/Cell_biology "Cell biology").[[278]](#cite_note-279) [Lazăr Edeleanu](/wiki/Laz%C4%83r_Edeleanu "Lazăr Edeleanu") was the first chemist to synthesise [amphetamine](/wiki/Amphetamine "Amphetamine"), and he also invented the procedure of separating valuable petroleum components with selective solvents.[[279]](#cite_note-280)

During the 1990s and 2000s, the development of research was hampered by several factors, including corruption, low funding, and a considerable [brain drain](/wiki/Brain_drain "Brain drain").[[280]](#cite_note-281) In recent years, Romania has ranked the lowest or second-lowest in the EU by [research and development](/wiki/Research_and_development "Research and development") spending as a percentage of GDP, standing at roughly 0.5% in 2016 and 2017, substantially below the EU average of just over 2%.[[281]](#cite_note-282)[[282]](#cite_note-283) The country joined the [European Space Agency](/wiki/European_Space_Agency "European Space Agency") (ESA) in 2011,[[283]](#cite_note-284) and [CERN](/wiki/CERN "CERN") in 2016.[[284]](#cite_note-285) In 2018, however, Romania lost its voting rights in the ESA due to a failure to pay €56.8 million in membership contributions to the agency.[[285]](#cite_note-286)

In the early 2010s, the situation for science in Romania was characterised as "rapidly improving" albeit from a low base.[[286]](#cite_note-287) In January 2011, Parliament passed a law that enforces "strict quality control on universities and introduces tough rules for funding evaluation and peer review".[[287]](#cite_note-Abbott2011-288) Romania was ranked 49th in the [Global Innovation Index](/wiki/Global_Innovation_Index "Global Innovation Index") in 2025.[[288]](#cite_note-289)[[289]](#cite_note-290)

The [nuclear physics](/wiki/Nuclear_physics "Nuclear physics") facility of the EU's proposed [Extreme Light Infrastructure](/wiki/Extreme_Light_Infrastructure "Extreme Light Infrastructure") (ELI) [laser](/wiki/Laser "Laser") will be built in Romania.[[290]](#cite_note-291) In early 2012, Romania launched its first [satellite](/wiki/Goliat "Goliat") from the [Centre Spatial Guyanais](/wiki/Centre_Spatial_Guyanais "Centre Spatial Guyanais") in French Guiana.[[291]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-292) Starting in December 2014, Romania became a co-owner of the [International Space Station](/wiki/International_Space_Station "International Space Station").[[292]](#cite_note-autogenerated4-293)

## Demographics

Main articles: [Demographics of Romania](/wiki/Demographics_of_Romania "Demographics of Romania") and [Romanians](/wiki/Romanians "Romanians")

See also: [Demographic history of Romania](/wiki/Demographic_history_of_Romania "Demographic history of Romania"), [Immigration to Romania](/wiki/Immigration_to_Romania "Immigration to Romania"), and [Minorities in Romania](/wiki/Minorities_in_Romania "Minorities in Romania")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Rom%C3%A2nii_pe_jude%C8%9Be_%28Rom%C3%A2nia%2C_1930-2021%29.jpg/250px-Rom%C3%A2nii_pe_jude%C8%9Be_%28Rom%C3%A2nia%2C_1930-2021%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rom%C3%A2nii_pe_jude%C8%9Be_(Rom%C3%A2nia,_1930-2021).jpg)

Romanians by counties (Ethnic maps 1930–2021)

According to the [2021 Romanian census](/wiki/2021_Romanian_census "2021 Romanian census"), Romania's population was 19,053,815.[[5]](#cite_note-Census2021-5) Like other countries in the region, its population is expected to decline gradually as a result of [sub-replacement fertility rates](/wiki/Sub-replacement_fertility_rate "Sub-replacement fertility rate") and negative [net migration rate](/wiki/Net_migration_rate "Net migration rate"). According to the 2021 Romanian census, [Romanians](/wiki/Romanians "Romanians") made up 89.33% of the population, [Hungarians](/wiki/Hungarians "Hungarians") 6.05% and the [Roma](/wiki/Romani_people "Romani people") 3.44% of the population,[[5]](#cite_note-Census2021-5) but many ethnicities are not recorded, as they do not have ID cards.[[293]](#cite_note-294) International sources give higher figures for Roma than the official census.[[294]](#cite_note-295)[[295]](#cite_note-296)[[296]](#cite_note-297) According to the [Council of Europe](/wiki/Council_of_Europe "Council of Europe"), the [Roma](/wiki/Romani_people "Romani people") make up 8.32% of the population;[[297]](#cite_note-298) this figure is difficult to verify due to the mobility of Roma and the reluctance of some of them to disclose their ethnicity.[[298]](#cite_note-299) Hungarians constitute a majority in the counties of [Harghita](/wiki/Harghita "Harghita") and [Covasna](/wiki/Covasna_County "Covasna County"). Other minorities include [Ukrainians](/wiki/Ukrainians_of_Romania "Ukrainians of Romania"), [Germans](/wiki/Germans_of_Romania "Germans of Romania"), [Turks](/wiki/Turks_of_Romania "Turks of Romania"), [Lipovans](/wiki/Lipovans "Lipovans"), [Aromanians](/wiki/Aromanians_in_Romania "Aromanians in Romania"), [Tatars](/wiki/Tatars_of_Romania "Tatars of Romania"), and [Serbs](/wiki/Serbs_of_Romania "Serbs of Romania").[[299]](#cite_note-census-300) In 1930, there were 745,421 Germans living in Romania,[[300]](#cite_note-301) but only about 36,000 remained in the country to this day.[[299]](#cite_note-census-300) As of 2009[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Romania&action=edit), there were also approximately 133,000 immigrants living in Romania, primarily from Moldova and China.[[161]](#cite_note-hdrstats.undp.org-162)

The [total fertility rate](/wiki/Total_fertility_rate "Total fertility rate") (TFR) in 2018 was estimated at 1.36 children born per woman, which is below the replacement rate of 2.1, and one of the lowest in the world;[[301]](#cite_note-cia.gov1-302) it remains considerably below the high of 5.82 children born per woman in 1912.[[302]](#cite_note-303) In 2014, 31.2% of births were to unmarried women.[[303]](#cite_note-304)
The [birth rate](/wiki/Birth_rate "Birth rate") (9.49‰, 2012) is much lower than the [mortality rate](/wiki/Mortality_rate "Mortality rate") (11.84‰, 2012), resulting in a shrinking (−0.26% per year, 2012) and aging population (median age: 41.6 years, 2018), one of the oldest populations in the world,[[301]](#cite_note-cia.gov1-302) with approximately 16.8% of total population aged 65 years and over.[[301]](#cite_note-cia.gov1-302)[[304]](#cite_note-populationdata.net-305)[[305]](#cite_note-306) The life expectancy in 2015 was estimated at 74.92 years (71.46 years male, 78.59 years female).[[306]](#cite_note-cia.gov-307)
The number of Romanians and individuals with ancestors born in Romania living abroad is estimated at 12 million.[[307]](#cite_note-diaspora-308) After the [Romanian Revolution of 1989](/wiki/Romanian_Revolution_of_1989 "Romanian Revolution of 1989"), a significant number of Romanians emigrated to other European countries, North America or Australia.[[308]](#cite_note-309) For example, in 1990, 96,919 Romanians permanently settled abroad.[[309]](#cite_note-310)

### Languages

Main articles: [Romanian language](/wiki/Romanian_language "Romanian language") and [Languages of Romania](/wiki/Languages_of_Romania "Languages of Romania")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/Limba_romana_Atlanticul-800px.png/250px-Limba_romana_Atlanticul-800px.png)](/wiki/File:Limba_romana_Atlanticul-800px.png)

Map highlighting the use of the Romanian language worldwide, both as a native and as a foreign language

The official language is Romanian, a [Romance language](/wiki/Romance_language "Romance language") (the most widely spoken of the [Eastern Romance branch](/wiki/Eastern_Romance_languages "Eastern Romance languages")), which presents a consistent degree of similarity to [Aromanian](/wiki/Aromanian_language "Aromanian language"), [Megleno-Romanian](/wiki/Megleno-Romanian_language "Megleno-Romanian language"), and [Istro-Romanian](/wiki/Istro-Romanian_language "Istro-Romanian language"), but shares many features equally with the rest of the [Western Romance languages](/wiki/Western_Romance_languages "Western Romance languages"), specifically Italian, French, Spanish, [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"), and [Catalan](/wiki/Catalan_language "Catalan language").[[310]](#cite_note-:1-311) The [Romanian alphabet](/wiki/Romanian_alphabet "Romanian alphabet") contains the same 26 letters of the standard Latin alphabet, as well as five additional ones (namely *ă*, *â*, *î*, *ț*, and *ș*), totalling 31.[[310]](#cite_note-:1-311)

Romanian is spoken as a first language by 91.55% of the entire population, while [Hungarian](/wiki/Hungarian_language "Hungarian language") and [Vlax Romani](/wiki/Vlax_Romani "Vlax Romani") are spoken by 6.28% and 1.20% of the population, respectively. There are also 40,861 native speakers of [Ukrainian](/wiki/Ukrainian_language "Ukrainian language") (concentrated in some compact regions near the border, where they form local majorities),[[311]](#cite_note-infomm.ro_2015-05-05-312) 17,101 native speakers of [Turkish](/wiki/Turkish_language "Turkish language"), 15,943 native speakers of [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"), and 14,414 native speakers of [Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language") living in Romania.[[312]](#cite_note-Census2021-Language-313)[[313]](#cite_note-census_2011_lang-314)

According to the Constitution, local councils ensure linguistic rights to all minorities. In localities with ethnic minorities of over 20%, that minority's language can be used in the public administration, justice system, and education. Foreign citizens and stateless persons who live in Romania have access to justice and education in their own language.[[314]](#cite_note-315) English and French are the main foreign languages taught in schools.[[315]](#cite_note-316) In 2010, the [Organisation internationale de la Francophonie](/wiki/Organisation_internationale_de_la_Francophonie "Organisation internationale de la Francophonie") identified 4,756,100 French speakers in the country.[[316]](#cite_note-317) According to the 2012 [Eurobarometer](/wiki/Eurobarometer "Eurobarometer"), English is spoken by 31% of Romanians, French is spoken by 17%, and Italian and German, each by 7%.[[317]](#cite_note-318)

### Religion

Main articles: [Religion in Romania](/wiki/Religion_in_Romania "Religion in Romania") and [Romanian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Romanian_Orthodox_Church "Romanian Orthodox Church")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Catedrala_M%C3%A2ntuirii_Neamului_%28Octombrie_2025%29.jpg/250px-Catedrala_M%C3%A2ntuirii_Neamului_%28Octombrie_2025%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Catedrala_M%C3%A2ntuirii_Neamului_(Octombrie_2025).jpg)

[The People's Salvation Cathedral](/wiki/National_Cathedral_of_Romania "National Cathedral of Romania") in [Bucharest](/wiki/Bucharest "Bucharest") is the [tallest](/wiki/List_of_tallest_Eastern_Orthodox_church_buildings "List of tallest Eastern Orthodox church buildings") and [largest Eastern Orthodox church building](/wiki/List_of_largest_church_buildings "List of largest church buildings")[[b]](#cite_note-Isaac.Cathedral-320) in the world[[319]](#cite_note-321)

Romania is a [secular state](/wiki/Secular_state "Secular state") and has no [state religion](/wiki/State_religion "State religion"). An overwhelming majority of the population identify themselves as Christians. At the country's 2021 census,[[2]](#cite_note-Census2021-Religion-2) 73.60% of respondents identified as [Orthodox Christians](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church"), with 73.42% belonging to the [Romanian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Romanian_Orthodox_Church "Romanian Orthodox Church"). Other denominations include [Protestantism](/wiki/Religion_in_Romania#Protestantism "Religion in Romania") (6.22%), [Roman Catholicism](/wiki/Roman_Catholicism_in_Romania "Roman Catholicism in Romania") (3.89%), and [Greek Catholicism](/wiki/Romanian_Greek_Catholic_Church "Romanian Greek Catholic Church") (0.61%). From the remaining population 128,291 people belong to other Christian denominations or have another religion, which includes 58,347 [Muslims](/wiki/Islam_in_Romania "Islam in Romania") (mostly of Turkish and Tatar ethnicity) and 2,708 [Jewish](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Romania "History of the Jews in Romania") (Jews once constituted 4% of the Romanian population—728,115 persons in the 1930 census). Additionally, 71,430 people are [irreligious](/wiki/Irreligious "Irreligious"), 57,229 are [atheist](/wiki/Atheist "Atheist"), 25,485 are [agnostic](/wiki/Agnostic "Agnostic"), and 2,658,165 people chose to not declare their religion.[[2]](#cite_note-Census2021-Religion-2)

The Romanian Orthodox Church is an [autocephalous](/wiki/Autocephalous "Autocephalous") [Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") in [full communion](/wiki/Full_communion "Full communion") with other Orthodox churches, with a [Patriarch](/wiki/Patriarch_of_All_Romania "Patriarch of All Romania") as its leader. It is the third-largest Eastern Orthodox Church in the world,[[320]](#cite_note-322) and unlike other Orthodox churches, it functions within a [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") culture and uses a [Romance](/wiki/Romance_languages "Romance languages") liturgical language.[[321]](#cite_note-323) Its canonical jurisdiction covers the territories of Romania and Moldova.[[322]](#cite_note-324) Romania has the [world's third-largest Eastern Orthodox population](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodoxy_by_country "Eastern Orthodoxy by country").[[323]](#cite_note-325)[[324]](#cite_note-326)

### Urbanisation

Main articles: [List of cities and towns in Romania](/wiki/List_of_cities_and_towns_in_Romania "List of cities and towns in Romania") and [Metropolitan areas in Romania](/wiki/Metropolitan_areas_in_Romania "Metropolitan areas in Romania")

Although 54.0% of the population lived in [urban areas](/wiki/Urban_areas "Urban areas") in 2011,[[325]](#cite_note-Census2011-327) this percentage has been declining since 1996.[[326]](#cite_note-328) Counties with over 2⁄3 urban population are [Hunedoara](/wiki/Hunedoara_County "Hunedoara County"), [Brașov](/wiki/Bra%C8%99ov_County "Brașov County") and [Constanța](/wiki/Constan%C8%9Ba_County "Constanța County"), while those with less than a third are [Dâmbovița](/wiki/D%C3%A2mbovi%C8%9Ba_County "Dâmbovița County") (30.06%) and [Giurgiu](/wiki/Giurgiu_County "Giurgiu County") and [Teleorman](/wiki/Teleorman_County "Teleorman County").[[325]](#cite_note-Census2011-327) Bucharest is the capital and the largest city in Romania, with a population of over 1.7 million in 2021.[[327]](#cite_note-RPL2021-329) Its [larger urban zone](/wiki/Larger_urban_zone "Larger urban zone") has a population of almost 2.2 million,[[328]](#cite_note-330) which are planned to be included into a [metropolitan area](/wiki/Bucharest_metropolitan_area "Bucharest metropolitan area") up to 20 times the area of the [city proper](/wiki/City_proper "City proper").[[329]](#cite_note-331)[[330]](#cite_note-332)[[331]](#cite_note-333)

Another 17 cities have a population of over 100,000, with Cluj-Napoca, Iași, Constanța and Timișoara having more than 250,000 inhabitants, and Craiova, Brașov and Galați with over 200,000 inhabitants.[[327]](#cite_note-RPL2021-329) [Metropolitan areas](/wiki/Metropolitan_areas_in_Romania "Metropolitan areas in Romania") have been constituted for most of these cities.

### Education

Main article: [Education in Romania](/wiki/Education_in_Romania "Education in Romania")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/Universitatea_din_Bucuresti_din_Piata_Universitatii.jpg/250px-Universitatea_din_Bucuresti_din_Piata_Universitatii.jpg)](/wiki/File:Universitatea_din_Bucuresti_din_Piata_Universitatii.jpg)

The [University of Bucharest](/wiki/University_of_Bucharest "University of Bucharest") was opened in 1864.

Since the [Romanian revolution](/wiki/Romanian_revolution "Romanian revolution") of 1989, the Romanian educational system has been in a continuous process of reform that has received mixed criticism.[[333]](#cite_note-335) In 2004, some 4.4 million individuals were enrolled in school. Of these, 650,000 were in [kindergarten](/wiki/Kindergarten "Kindergarten") (three-six years), 3.11 million in primary and secondary level, and 650,000 in tertiary level (universities).[[334]](#cite_note-336) In 2018, the adult literacy rate was 98.8%.[[335]](#cite_note-337) Kindergarten is optional between three and five years. Since 2020, compulsory schooling starts at age 5 with the last year of kindergarten (grupa mare) and is compulsory until twelfth grade.[[336]](#cite_note-338)[[337]](#cite_note-339) Primary and secondary education is divided into 12 or 13 grades. There is also a semi-legal, informal [private tutoring](/wiki/Tutoring#Peer_tutoring "Tutoring") system used mostly during secondary school, which prospered during the Communist regime.[[338]](#cite_note-340)

As of 2025, [Babeș-Bolyai University](/wiki/Babe%C8%99-Bolyai_University "Babeș-Bolyai University") of [Cluj-Napoca](/wiki/Cluj-Napoca "Cluj-Napoca") and the [University of Bucharest](/wiki/University_of_Bucharest "University of Bucharest") are included in the [QS World University Rankings](/wiki/QS_World_University_Rankings "QS World University Rankings")' top 800.[[339]](#cite_note-341)

Romania ranks fifth in the all-time medal count at the [International Mathematical Olympiad](/wiki/International_Mathematical_Olympiad "International Mathematical Olympiad") with 316 total medals, dating back to 1959. [Ciprian Manolescu](/wiki/Ciprian_Manolescu "Ciprian Manolescu") managed to write a perfect paper (42 points) for a gold medal more times than anybody else in the history of the competition, in 1995, 1996 and 1997.[[340]](#cite_note-342) Romania has achieved the highest team score in the competition, after China, Russia, the United States and Hungary. Romania also ranks sixth in the all-time medal count at the [International Olympiad in Informatics](/wiki/International_Olympiad_in_Informatics "International Olympiad in Informatics") with 107 total medals, dating back to 1989.[[341]](#cite_note-343)[[342]](#cite_note-344)[[343]](#cite_note-345)

### Healthcare

Main article: [Healthcare in Romania](/wiki/Healthcare_in_Romania "Healthcare in Romania")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/Bucharest_-_Col%C8%9Bea_Hospital.jpg/250px-Bucharest_-_Col%C8%9Bea_Hospital.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bucharest_-_Col%C8%9Bea_Hospital.jpg)

The Colțea Hospital in Bucharest completed a $90 million renovation in 2011[[344]](#cite_note-346)

Healthcare in Romania is mainly provided by the public sector, which runs most hospitals and offers national health insurance to nearly all citizens. In 2021, healthcare costs were US$16. 7 billion, or US$2,385 per person, making up €5.69 of GDP. Government spending is higher than in markets like Bulgaria but lower than Hungary. Spending is expected to rise by 7. US$5 billion (+37. 68%) from 2024 to 2028, reaching 27. US$3 billion by 2028.[[345]](#cite_note-w828-347)

The Romanian National Institute of Statistics reports over 65,000 health units in Romania, with 53,000 in urban areas and 12,000 in rural areas. There are 543 hospitals, including 488 in urban and 55 in rural areas, along with 160 other hospital-like establishments. Nearly 50% of these are large facilities with over 100 beds, while 39% are small with fewer than 50 beds. The total number of inpatient beds is 135,085, allocated mainly to psychiatry, surgery, and internal medicine among other specialties.[[346]](#cite_note-i223-348)

**Architecture**

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Sibiuphoto.jpg/250px-Sibiuphoto.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sibiuphoto.jpg)

[Sibiu](/wiki/Sibiu "Sibiu") was the 2007 [European Capital of Culture](/wiki/European_Capital_of_Culture "European Capital of Culture") and the 2019 [European Region of Gastronomy](/wiki/European_Region_of_Gastronomy "European Region of Gastronomy").

The topic of the origin of Romanian culture began to be discussed by the end of the 18th century among the [Transylvanian School](/wiki/Transylvanian_School "Transylvanian School") scholars.[[347]](#cite_note-iciculture-349) Several writers rose to prominence in the 19th century, including: [George Coșbuc](/wiki/George_Co%C8%99buc "George Coșbuc"), [Ioan Slavici](/wiki/Ioan_Slavici "Ioan Slavici"), [Mihail Kogălniceanu](/wiki/Mihail_Kog%C4%83lniceanu "Mihail Kogălniceanu"), [Vasile Alecsandri](/wiki/Vasile_Alecsandri "Vasile Alecsandri"), [Nicolae Bălcescu](/wiki/Nicolae_B%C4%83lcescu "Nicolae Bălcescu"), [Ion Luca Caragiale](/wiki/Ion_Luca_Caragiale "Ion Luca Caragiale"), [Ion Creangă](/wiki/Ion_Creang%C4%83 "Ion Creangă"), and [Mihai Eminescu](/wiki/Mihai_Eminescu "Mihai Eminescu"), the later being considered the greatest and most influential Romanian poet, particularly for the poem *[Luceafărul](/wiki/Luceaf%C4%83rul_(poem) "Luceafărul (poem)")*.[[348]](#cite_note-350)

In the 20th century, a number of Romanian artists and writers achieved international acclaim, including: [Tristan Tzara](/wiki/Tristan_Tzara "Tristan Tzara"), [Marcel Janco](/wiki/Marcel_Janco "Marcel Janco"),[[349]](#cite_note-351) [Mircea Eliade](/wiki/Mircea_Eliade "Mircea Eliade"), [Nicolae Grigorescu](/wiki/Nicolae_Grigorescu "Nicolae Grigorescu"), [Marin Preda](/wiki/Marin_Preda "Marin Preda"), [Liviu Rebreanu](/wiki/Liviu_Rebreanu "Liviu Rebreanu"),[[350]](#cite_note-352) [Eugène Ionesco](/wiki/Eug%C3%A8ne_Ionesco "Eugène Ionesco"), [Emil Cioran](/wiki/Emil_Cioran "Emil Cioran"), and [Constantin Brâncuși](/wiki/Constantin_Br%C3%A2ncu%C8%99i "Constantin Brâncuși"). Brâncuși has a sculptural ensemble in Târgu Jiu, while his sculpture *[Bird in Space](/wiki/Bird_in_Space "Bird in Space")*, was auctioned in 2005 for $27.5 million.[[351]](#cite_note-353)[[352]](#cite_note-354) Romanian-born Holocaust survivor [Elie Wiesel](/wiki/Elie_Wiesel "Elie Wiesel") received the [Nobel Peace Prize](/wiki/Nobel_Peace_Prize "Nobel Peace Prize") in 1986, while [Banat Swabian](/wiki/Banat_Swabian "Banat Swabian") writer [Herta Müller](/wiki/Herta_M%C3%BCller "Herta Müller") received the 2009 [Nobel Prize in Literature](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Literature "Nobel Prize in Literature").[[353]](#cite_note-355)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/Universitatea_Politehnica_Timisoara_-_Rectorat.jpg/250px-Universitatea_Politehnica_Timisoara_-_Rectorat.jpg)](/wiki/File:Universitatea_Politehnica_Timisoara_-_Rectorat.jpg)

[Timișoara](/wiki/Timi%C8%99oara "Timișoara") was designated the [European Capital of Culture](/wiki/European_Capital_of_Culture "European Capital of Culture") in 2021 and held this title in 2023 due to [COVID-19](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic") postponement.

Prominent Romanian painters include: [Nicolae Grigorescu](/wiki/Nicolae_Grigorescu "Nicolae Grigorescu"), [Ștefan Luchian](/wiki/%C8%98tefan_Luchian "Ștefan Luchian"), [Ion Andreescu](/wiki/Ion_Andreescu "Ion Andreescu") [Nicolae Tonitza](/wiki/Nicolae_Tonitza "Nicolae Tonitza"), and [Theodor Aman](/wiki/Theodor_Aman "Theodor Aman"). Notable Romanian classical composers of the 19th and 20th centuries include: [Ciprian Porumbescu](/wiki/Ciprian_Porumbescu "Ciprian Porumbescu"), [Anton Pann](/wiki/Anton_Pann "Anton Pann"), [Eduard Caudella](/wiki/Eduard_Caudella "Eduard Caudella"), [Mihail Jora](/wiki/Mihail_Jora "Mihail Jora"), [Dinu Lipatti](/wiki/Dinu_Lipatti "Dinu Lipatti"), and especially [George Enescu](/wiki/George_Enescu "George Enescu"). The annual [George Enescu Festival](/wiki/George_Enescu_Festival "George Enescu Festival") is held in Bucharest in honour of the 20th-century composer.[[354]](#cite_note-356)

Contemporary musicians like [Angela Gheorghiu](/wiki/Angela_Gheorghiu "Angela Gheorghiu"), [Gheorghe Zamfir](/wiki/Gheorghe_Zamfir "Gheorghe Zamfir"),[[355]](#cite_note-357)[[356]](#cite_note-358) [Inna](/wiki/Inna "Inna"),[[357]](#cite_note-359) [Alexandra Stan](/wiki/Alexandra_Stan "Alexandra Stan"),[[358]](#cite_note-360) and many others have achieved various levels of international acclaim. From the late 2000s through the early 2010s, the Romanian [popcorn](/wiki/Popcorn_(Romanian_music_style) "Popcorn (Romanian music style)") music style had established itself in the international mainstream.[[359]](#cite_note-361)[[360]](#cite_note-362) At the [Eurovision Song Contest](/wiki/Eurovision_Song_Contest "Eurovision Song Contest") Romanian singers achieved third place in 2005 and 2010.[[361]](#cite_note-363)

In cinema, several movies of the [Romanian New Wave](/wiki/Romanian_New_Wave "Romanian New Wave") have achieved international acclaim. At the [Cannes Film Festival](/wiki/Cannes_Film_Festival "Cannes Film Festival"), *[The Death of Mr. Lazarescu](/wiki/The_Death_of_Mr._Lazarescu "The Death of Mr. Lazarescu")* by [Cristi Puiu](/wiki/Cristi_Puiu "Cristi Puiu") won the *[Prix Un Certain Regard](/wiki/Un_Certain_Regard#Prix_Un_Certain_Regard_winners "Un Certain Regard")* in 2005,[[362]](#cite_note-364) while *[4 Months, 3 Weeks and 2 Days](/wiki/4_Months,_3_Weeks_and_2_Days "4 Months, 3 Weeks and 2 Days")* by [Cristian Mungiu](/wiki/Cristian_Mungiu "Cristian Mungiu") won the festival's top prize, the *[Palme d'Or](/wiki/Palme_d%27Or "Palme d'Or")*, in 2007.[[363]](#cite_note-365) At the [Berlin International Film Festival](/wiki/Berlin_International_Film_Festival "Berlin International Film Festival"), *[Child's Pose](/wiki/Child%27s_Pose_(film) "Child's Pose (film)")* by [Călin Peter Netzer](/wiki/C%C4%83lin_Peter_Netzer "Călin Peter Netzer") won the [Golden Bear](/wiki/Golden_Bear "Golden Bear") in 2013.[[364]](#cite_note-366)

The list of World Heritage Sites includes [six cultural sites](/wiki/List_of_World_Heritage_Sites_in_Romania "List of World Heritage Sites in Romania") located within Romania, including eight [painted churches of northern Moldavia](/wiki/Painted_churches_of_northern_Moldavia "Painted churches of northern Moldavia"), eight wooden churches of [Maramureș](/wiki/Maramure%C8%99 "Maramureș"), seven villages with fortified churches in Transylvania, the [Horezu Monastery](/wiki/Horezu_Monastery "Horezu Monastery"), and the [Historic Centre of Sighișoara](/wiki/Historic_Centre_of_Sighi%C8%99oara "Historic Centre of Sighișoara").[[365]](#cite_note-367) The city of Sibiu, with its [Brukenthal National Museum](/wiki/Brukenthal_National_Museum "Brukenthal National Museum"), was selected as the 2007 [European Capital of Culture](/wiki/European_Capital_of_Culture "European Capital of Culture") and the 2019 [European Region of Gastronomy](/wiki/European_Region_of_Gastronomy "European Region of Gastronomy").[[366]](#cite_note-368)[[367]](#cite_note-369) Multiple castles exist in Romania, including the popular tourist attractions of [Peleș Castle](/wiki/Pele%C8%99_Castle "Peleș Castle"),[[368]](#cite_note-370) [Corvin Castle](/wiki/Corvin_Castle "Corvin Castle"), and Bran Castle or "Dracula's Castle".[[369]](#cite_note-371)

### Holidays, traditions, and cuisine

See also: [Romanian dress](/wiki/Romanian_dress "Romanian dress"), [Folklore of Romania](/wiki/Folklore_of_Romania "Folklore of Romania"), and [Romanian cuisine](/wiki/Romanian_cuisine "Romanian cuisine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Sibiu_Christmas_Market_opening_2008.JPG/250px-Sibiu_Christmas_Market_opening_2008.JPG)](/wiki/File:Sibiu_Christmas_Market_opening_2008.JPG)

The Christmas market in [Sibiu](/wiki/Sibiu "Sibiu") is one of the most famous in Europe.

There are 12 non-working public holidays, including the [Great Union Day](/wiki/Great_Union_Day "Great Union Day"), celebrated on 1 December in commemoration of the 1918 union of Transylvania with Romania.[[370]](#cite_note-372) Winter holidays include the [Christmas](/wiki/Christmas "Christmas") and New Year festivities during which various unique folklore dances and games are common: *[plugușorul](/wiki/Plugu%C8%99orul "Plugușorul")*, *[sorcova](/wiki/Sorcova "Sorcova")*, *ursul*, and *capra*.[[371]](#cite_note-373)[[372]](#cite_note-374) The traditional [Romanian dress](/wiki/Romanian_dress "Romanian dress") that otherwise has largely fallen out of use during the 20th century, is a popular ceremonial vestment worn on these festivities, especially in rural areas.[[373]](#cite_note-375) There are sacrifices of live pigs during Christmas and lambs during Easter that has required a special exemption from EU law after 2007.[[374]](#cite_note-376) In the Easter, traditions such as [painting the eggs](/wiki/Easter_egg "Easter egg") are very common. On 1 March **[mărțișor](/wiki/M%C4%83r%C8%9Bi%C8%99or "Mărțișor")** gifting is featured, which is a tradition whereby females are gifted with a type of talisman that is given for good luck.[[375]](#cite_note-377)

Romanian cuisine has been influenced by [Austrian](/wiki/Austrian_cuisine "Austrian cuisine") and [German cuisine](/wiki/German_cuisine "German cuisine") (especially in the historical regions that had been formerly administered by the [Habsburg monarchy](/wiki/Habsburg_monarchy "Habsburg monarchy")), but also shares some similarities with other cuisines in the [Balkan region](/wiki/Balkans "Balkans") such as [Greek](/wiki/Greek_cuisine "Greek cuisine"), [Bulgarian](/wiki/Bulgarian_cuisine "Bulgarian cuisine"), and [Serbian cuisine](/wiki/Serbian_cuisine "Serbian cuisine").[[376]](#cite_note-378) *[Ciorbă](/wiki/Ciorb%C4%83 "Ciorbă")* includes a wide range of [sour soups](/wiki/Sour_soup "Sour soup"), while **[mititei](/wiki/Mititei "Mititei")**, **[mămăligă](/wiki/M%C4%83m%C4%83lig%C4%83 "Mămăligă")** (similar to [polenta](/wiki/Polenta "Polenta")), and *[sarmale](/wiki/Sarma_(food) "Sarma (food)")* are featured commonly in main courses.[[377]](#cite_note-379)

Pork, chicken, and beef are the preferred types of meat, but lamb and fish are also quite popular.[[378]](#cite_note-380)[[379]](#cite_note-381) Certain traditional recipes are made in direct connection with the holidays: **[chiftele](/wiki/Kofta "Kofta")**, **[tobă](/wiki/Tob%C4%83 "Tobă")** and **[tochitură](/wiki/Tochitur%C4%83 "Tochitură")** at [Christmas](/wiki/Christmas "Christmas"); **[drob](/wiki/Drob "Drob")**, **[pască](/wiki/Paska_(bread) "Paska (bread)")** and **[cozonac](/wiki/Cozonac "Cozonac")** at Easter and other Romanian holidays.[[380]](#cite_note-382) *[Țuică](/wiki/%C8%9Auic%C4%83 "Țuică")* is a strong plum [brandy](/wiki/Brandy "Brandy") reaching a 70% alcohol content which is the country's traditional alcoholic beverage, taking as much as 75% of the national crop (Romania is one of the [largest plum producers in the world](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_plum_production "List of countries by plum production")).[[381]](#cite_note-383)[[382]](#cite_note-educations-384) Traditional alcoholic beverages also include [wine](/wiki/Romanian_wine "Romanian wine"), *[rachiu](/wiki/Rachiu "Rachiu")*, *[palincă](/wiki/Palinc%C4%83 "Palincă")* and *[vișinată](/wiki/Vi%C8%99inat%C4%83 "Vișinată")*, but [beer](/wiki/Beer_in_Romania "Beer in Romania") consumption has increased dramatically over recent years.[[383]](#cite_note-385)

### Media

### Sports

Main article: [Sport in Romania](/wiki/Sport_in_Romania "Sport in Romania")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Stamps_of_Romania%2C_2011-75.jpg/250px-Stamps_of_Romania%2C_2011-75.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stamps_of_Romania,_2011-75.jpg)

[Arena Națională](/wiki/Arena_Na%C8%9Bional%C4%83 "Arena Națională"), opened in 2011, the national stadium of Romania, as seen on a Romanian stamp (2011)

[Football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") is the most popular sport in Romania with over 219,000 registered players as of 2018[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Romania&action=edit). The market for professional football in Romania is roughly €740 million according to [UEFA](/wiki/UEFA "UEFA").[[384]](#cite_note-386)

The governing body is the [Romanian Football Federation](/wiki/Romanian_Football_Federation "Romanian Football Federation"), which belongs to UEFA. The [Romania national football team](/wiki/Romania_national_football_team "Romania national football team") played its first match in 1922 and is one of only four national teams to have taken part in the first three [FIFA World Cups](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup "FIFA World Cup"), the other three being Brazil, France, and Belgium. Overall, it has played in seven World Cups and had its most successful period during the 1990s, when it finished 6th at the [1994 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/1994_FIFA_World_Cup "1994 FIFA World Cup"), eventually being ranked 3rd by [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA") in 1997.[[385]](#cite_note-387)

The most successful club is [Steaua București](/wiki/CSA_Steaua_Bucure%C8%99ti_(football) "CSA Steaua București (football)"), who were the first Eastern European team to win the [UEFA Champions League](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League") in 1986, and were runners-up in 1989.[[386]](#cite_note-388) [Dinamo București](/wiki/FC_Dinamo_Bucure%C8%99ti "FC Dinamo București") reached the UEFA Champions League semi-final in 1984 and the [UEFA Cup Winners' Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Cup_Winners%27_Cup "UEFA Cup Winners' Cup") semi-final in [1990](/wiki/1989%E2%80%9390_European_Cup_Winners%27_Cup "1989–90 European Cup Winners' Cup").[[387]](#cite_note-389) Other important Romanian football clubs are [Rapid București](/wiki/FC_Rapid_Bucure%C8%99ti "FC Rapid București"),[[388]](#cite_note-390) [UTA Arad](/wiki/UTA_Arad "UTA Arad"),[[389]](#cite_note-391) [Universitatea Craiova](/wiki/CS_Universitatea_Craiova "CS Universitatea Craiova"),[[390]](#cite_note-392) [Petrolul Ploiești](/wiki/Petrolul_Ploie%C8%99ti "Petrolul Ploiești"),[[391]](#cite_note-393) [CFR Cluj](/wiki/CFR_Cluj "CFR Cluj"),[[392]](#cite_note-394) [Astra Giurgiu](/wiki/Astra_Giurgiu "Astra Giurgiu"),[[393]](#cite_note-395) and [Viitorul Constanța](/wiki/Viitorul_Constan%C8%9Ba "Viitorul Constanța")[[394]](#cite_note-396) (the latter having recently merged with [FCV Farul Constanța](/wiki/FCV_Farul_Constan%C8%9Ba "FCV Farul Constanța")).[[395]](#cite_note-397)

Tennis is the second most popular sport.[[396]](#cite_note-Hotnews-398) Romania reached the [Davis Cup](/wiki/Davis_Cup "Davis Cup") finals three times in 1969, 1971 and 1972.[[397]](#cite_note-399) The second most popular [team sport](/wiki/Team_sport "Team sport") is [handball](/wiki/Handball "Handball").[[396]](#cite_note-Hotnews-398) The [men's team](/wiki/Romania_men%27s_national_handball_team "Romania men's national handball team") won the [handball world championship](/wiki/World_Men%27s_Handball_Championship "World Men's Handball Championship") in [1961](/wiki/1961_World_Men%27s_Handball_Championship "1961 World Men's Handball Championship"), [1964](/wiki/1964_World_Men%27s_Handball_Championship "1964 World Men's Handball Championship"), [1970](/wiki/1970_World_Men%27s_Handball_Championship "1970 World Men's Handball Championship"), [1974](/wiki/1974_World_Men%27s_Handball_Championship "1974 World Men's Handball Championship") making them the third most successful nation ever in the tournament. The [women's team](/wiki/Romania_women%27s_national_handball_team "Romania women's national handball team") won the [world championship](/wiki/IHF_World_Women%27s_Handball_Championship "IHF World Women's Handball Championship") in [1962](/wiki/1962_World_Women%27s_Handball_Championship "1962 World Women's Handball Championship") and have enjoyed more success than their male counterparts in recent years. In the club competition Romanian teams have won the [EHF Champions League](/wiki/EHF_Champions_League "EHF Champions League") a total of three times, [Steaua București](/wiki/CSA_Steaua_Bucure%C8%99ti_(handball) "CSA Steaua București (handball)") won in [1968](/wiki/1967%E2%80%9368_European_Cup_(handball) "1967–68 European Cup (handball)") as well as [1977](/wiki/1976%E2%80%9377_European_Cup_(handball) "1976–77 European Cup (handball)") and [Dinamo București](/wiki/CS_Dinamo_Bucure%C8%99ti_(men%27s_handball) "CS Dinamo București (men's handball)") won in [1965](/wiki/1964%E2%80%9365_European_Cup_(handball) "1964–65 European Cup (handball)").[[398]](#cite_note-400) In women's handball, powerhouse [CSM București](/wiki/CSM_Bucure%C8%99ti_(women%27s_handball) "CSM București (women's handball)") lifted the [EHF Champions League](/wiki/Women%27s_EHF_Champions_League "Women's EHF Champions League") trophy in 2016.[[399]](#cite_note-401)

Popular [individual sports](/wiki/Individual_sport "Individual sport") include [combat sports](/wiki/Combat_sport "Combat sport"),[[396]](#cite_note-Hotnews-398) [martial arts](/wiki/Martial_arts "Martial arts"),[[396]](#cite_note-Hotnews-398) and swimming.[[396]](#cite_note-Hotnews-398) In [professional boxing](/wiki/Professional_boxing "Professional boxing"), Romania has produced many world champions across the weight divisions internationally recognised by governing bodies.[[400]](#cite_note-402) Another popular combat sport is [professional kickboxing](/wiki/Professional_kickboxing "Professional kickboxing"), which has produced prominent practitioners including.[[401]](#cite_note-403)[[402]](#cite_note-404)

Romania's 306 [all-time Summer Olympics medals](/wiki/All-time_Olympic_Games_medal_table "All-time Olympic Games medal table") would rank 12th most among all countries, while its 89 gold medals would be 14th most. The [1984 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1984_Summer_Olympics "1984 Summer Olympics") was their most successful run, where they won 53 medals in total, 20 of them gold, ultimately placing 2nd to the hosts United States in the [medal rankings](/wiki/1984_Summer_Olympics_medal_table "1984 Summer Olympics medal table"). Amongst countries who have never hosted the event themselves, they are second in the total number of medals earned.[[403]](#cite_note-405) [Gymnastics](/wiki/Artistic_gymnastics "Artistic gymnastics") is the country's major medal-producing sport.[[404]](#cite_note-406)
